channel.title: arXiv keyword digest
channel.description: Daily arXiv papers summarized with Codex

item.title: Deep Gaussian Processes on Directed Acyclic Graphs
item.description:
Priority: 59/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 29, Paper 55, Empirical 68, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations are not supplied., Citation counts and h-index signals are not supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference venue is supplied., No code repository or license is mentioned., No public data availability statement is mentioned., No exact metrics, baseline list, ablations, or robustness/statistical tests are visible in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper, published 2026-07-10, with 75 pages and 14 figures.; Abstract proposes Deep Gaussian Processes over DAGs for partially observed noisy functions.; Includes theoretical lower bounds on information preservation and prior-collapse behavior.; Claims structured variational approximation preserving graph dependencies and collider explaining-away behavior.

TL;DR: The paper develops Deep Gaussian Processes over directed acyclic graphs to model partially observed compositional systems, with theory on prior collapse and a structured variational approximation for inference on DAG-dependent functions.

Problem: It studies reconstruction, uncertainty propagation, and inference when noisy, heterogeneously sampled observations are available only at some nodes of a DAG of composed functions, a setting arising in causal mechanisms, multi-fidelity engineering models, and biological regulatory networks.

Method: The authors place Gaussian-process priors over node functions in a DAG, yielding Deep Gaussian Processes on DAGs; they analyze information preservation under graph topology and intermediate observations, then propose a structured variational approximation that keeps graph dependencies and compositional uncertainty.

Key contribution: Compared with standard deep GPs or independent-node GP treatments, the paper explicitly incorporates arbitrary DAG topology, proves prior-collapse and information-preservation results tied to graph structure and input connections, and models collider-style explaining-away behavior in approximate inference.

Main result: From the abstract, the method is empirically validated on synthetic/theoretical settings plus a latent-collider DAG, a protein signalling network, and a multi-fidelity heavy-ion collision emulation task, where it reportedly attains state-of-the-art performance and recovers interpretable low-fidelity contributions.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide numerical metrics, baseline names, ablation results, robustness checks, code availability, data availability, affiliations, DOI, or peer-review status; the strength of the empirical and reproducibility claims cannot be fully assessed from the supplied metadata alone.

Relevance: This is relevant to the matched query "causality" because it treats DAG-composed functions as causal mechanisms and includes inference behavior for latent colliders, though its scope is broader than causal discovery alone.

Tags: deep Gaussian processes, DAGs, causality, structured variational inference, uncertainty propagation, multi-fidelity modeling, protein signalling

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper's framework or graphical-model figure showing GP priors assigned to functions on DAG nodes, including intermediate observations and collider dependencies.

Authors: Federico L. Perlino, Oliver Hamelijnck, Adam M. Johansen, Theodoros Damoulas

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: The Count Is There, but Misaligned: Understanding and Correcting Counting Failures in VLMs
item.description:
Priority: 58/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 28, Paper 45, Empirical 72, Reproducibility 45, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation counts or h-index signals, DOI or venue, model and dataset names, baseline details, ablation results

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-10 in cs.CV and cs.LG.; Abstract reports four VLMs and five counting datasets.; Uses nonlinear activation probes, SVCCA, and a causal steering intervention.; Reports up to 15.6 absolute percentage point counting accuracy improvement without parameter updates.

TL;DR: The paper argues that VLM counting failures often arise from a misalignment between internally encoded count information and verbalized answers, then uses activation probes, causal steering, and selective self-correction to improve counting accuracy.

Problem: It studies why vision-language models still fail at basic object counting, specifically whether failures reflect missing internal count knowledge or a failure to read out and express that knowledge correctly.

Method: The authors train simple activation probes on four VLMs across five counting datasets, compare ground-truth-count and model-output probe subspaces using SVCCA, test probe directions with a causal steering intervention, and propose detector-guided self-correction that re-prompts only when an internal error detector predicts failure.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is that correct count information can be present in VLM activations even when the model outputs the wrong answer, and that this can be causally leveraged through steering and a lightweight inference-time correction pipeline.

Main result: From the abstract, nonlinear probes reliably detect counting errors, causal steering improves counting performance, and detector-guided self-correction improves counting accuracy by up to 15.6 absolute percentage points without parameter updates.

Limitation: The abstract does not name the four VLMs, five datasets, baselines, ablations, code availability, statistical robustness, or failure cases, so empirical strength and reproducibility cannot be fully assessed from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: The paper matched the query "causality" because it uses a causal steering intervention on activation directions to validate that probe-identified count information has a causal effect on VLM counting outputs.

Tags: vision-language models, object counting, activation probing, causal steering, mechanistic interpretability, self-correction, inference-time intervention

Figure note: A pipeline or architecture figure showing activation probing, SVCCA alignment analysis, causal steering, and detector-guided self-correction would be useful, but no direct image URL is available from the supplied metadata.

Authors: Ahmed Oumar El-Shangiti, Abzal Nurgazy, Hilal AlQuabeh, Nikolai Rozanov, Kentaro Inui

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Misspecified regressions with mixed regressors: robust inference and causal interpretation
item.description:
Priority: 57/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 58, Paper 65, Empirical 20, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation counts, h-index or author impact metadata, DOI, journal or conference venue, open-source code

Score evidence: Abstract explicitly targets average treatment effect estimation in randomized experiments with fixed pretreatment covariates.; Claims a unified theory for regressions with both random and fixed components.; Allows misspecification of regression functions.; Analyzes misspecified linear regression and extends from independent to clustered data.

TL;DR: This paper develops asymptotic inference for misspecified regressions that combine randomized treatment variables with fixed pretreatment covariates, targeting causal interpretation of coefficients and standard errors in randomized experiments.

Problem: Existing regression inference frameworks usually assume either all regressors are random or all are fixed, leaving a gap for randomized experiments where treatment assignment is random but pretreatment covariates are fixed or non-randomized.

Method: The paper proposes a theory of estimating equations with mixed random and fixed components, then applies it to misspecified linear regression for completely randomized experiments, with extensions from independent data to clustered data.

Key contribution: The main novelty is a unified mixed-regressor framework that permits regression-function misspecification while preserving causal interpretation and robust inference for coefficients and standard errors.

Main result: The abstract reports general theoretical results for mixed-regressor estimating equations and their use in misspecified linear regression, but does not provide numerical empirical results, simulations, or benchmark comparisons.

Limitation: No code, data, simulations, empirical examples, DOI, venue, affiliations, citation metrics, or detailed assumptions are present in the supplied metadata/abstract; evidence is limited to the abstract-level theoretical claims.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the query "causality" because it directly studies average treatment effect estimation, randomized experiments, causal interpretation of regression coefficients, and robust standard errors under model misspecification.

Tags: causal inference, average treatment effect, randomized experiments, misspecified regression, mixed regressors, robust inference, clustered data, estimating equations

Figure note: A useful figure would show the proposed mixed-regressor inference framework: randomized treatment components, fixed pretreatment covariates, estimating equations, misspecified regression, and outputs for causal coefficient interpretation and robust standard errors.

Authors: Mengsi Gao, Peng Ding

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: How Does Bayesian Causal Discovery Fail? Characterising Structural Consequences in Linear Gaussian Networks under Latent Confounding
item.description:
Priority: 46/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 37, Paper 45, Empirical 58, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index signals, DOI or venue information, open-source code, public data or benchmark description, baselines

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-10 in cs.AI.; Focuses on Bayesian causal discovery under latent confounding in linear Gaussian causal models.; Derives a critical correlation threshold for when scores favor a spurious edge.; Reports exact posterior computations on multiple graph structures.

TL;DR: The paper analyzes how Bayesian causal discovery posteriors can systematically favor spurious DAG edges when observed variables are affected by latent confounding in linear Gaussian models.

Problem: Bayesian causal discovery quantifies uncertainty over DAGs, but its posterior behavior under latent confounding is not well characterized beyond the known fact that confounding breaks identifiability.

Method: The authors study linear Gaussian causal models with additive latent confounding between exactly two observed variables, derive a critical correlation threshold for when the score favors a spurious edge, and validate the predicted regimes using exact posterior computations on multiple graph structures.

Key contribution: The main novelty is a structural characterization of posterior failure under latent confounding, including a sample-size-dependent threshold and two local-structure-dependent failure regimes, rather than only stating that confounding causes non-identifiability.

Main result: The abstract reports that above a derived correlation threshold, Bayesian scores favor graphs with a spurious edge between the confounded variables, and that this threshold decreases as sample size increases; exact posterior computations support the predicted failure regimes.

Limitation: The analysis is limited, from the abstract, to linear Gaussian networks with additive latent confounding between exactly two observed variables; no code, broader model classes, real-data evidence, robustness analysis, or baselines are described in the supplied metadata.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causal discovery' because it studies a concrete failure mode of Bayesian causal discovery under latent confounding, a central challenge in causal structure learning.

Tags: causal discovery, Bayesian causal discovery, latent confounding, DAGs, linear Gaussian models, posterior inference, failure analysis

Figure note: A useful image would be a schematic showing the two observed variables with an unobserved confounder, the induced spurious edge in the learned DAG, and the threshold/failure-regime split by local graph structure.

Authors: Debargha Ghosh, Silja Renooij, Anna Kononova

Query: causal discovery, causality

PDF

item.title: Decision-analytical models as causal models
item.description:
Priority: 49/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 38, Paper 48, Empirical 20, Reproducibility 15, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index data, DOI or journal/conference venue, citation or influential-citation hints, open-source code, public data

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-10 in stat.ME.; Abstract explicitly frames health economic evaluations as causal questions targeting estimands over interventions.; The paper claims to formalize decision-analytical models as causal inference and decompose bias into model bias and target bias.; No DOI, journal reference, code, data, baselines, ablations, or empirical results are present in the supplied metadata.

TL;DR: The paper frames health-economic decision-analytical models as causal models and decomposes their bias into structural model bias and target-parameter bias.

Problem: Health economic evaluations require causal contrasts over costs and health outcomes under competing interventions, but the needed joint potential-outcome distributions are rarely observed in one data source and are often approximated through decision-analytical models.

Method: The authors formalize decision-analytical modeling as a causal inference task, treating model-implied outcome trajectories as expectations over potential outcomes informed by causal parameters synthesized from multiple sources.

Key contribution: The novel contribution is an explicit causal-inference foundation for decision-analytical models, including a decomposition of decision-analytical model bias into model-structure bias and input-parameter target bias, with emphasis on unconventional target parameters that lack direct observable analogues.

Main result: The abstract describes a conceptual/formal contribution rather than reporting empirical results, benchmarks, simulations, or validation metrics.

Limitation: The supplied abstract does not provide empirical validation, worked examples, code, data, sensitivity analyses, or evidence that the proposed bias decomposition improves real health-economic decisions.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' because it directly studies causal estimands, potential outcomes, causal assumptions, and bias in decision-analytical models used for medical decision making.

Tags: causal inference, health economics, decision-analytical models, potential outcomes, model bias, target bias, stat.ME

Figure note: A useful image would be a causal/decision-model pipeline diagram showing multiple evidence sources feeding causal target parameters, a decision-analytical model generating intervention-specific trajectories, and bias decomposed into model-structure bias and target bias.

Authors: Maurice Korf, Myriam Hunink, Richard Post, Jeremy Labrecque

Query: causal inference, causality

PDF

item.title: STEEL: Sparsity-Aware Fused Attention for Energy-Efficient Long-Sequence Inference on AMD's XDNA NPU
item.description:
Priority: 66/100 (Skim, confidence: high)

Quality dimensions: Author 38, Paper 68, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 58, Manuscript 76

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation count or h-index, DOI, code repository URL, dataset and benchmark details, sequence lengths and model sizes

Score evidence: Accepted at IEEE COINS 2026.; Claims first open-source FlashAttention implementation targeting XDNA-like NPUs.; Evaluated on AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370 SoC against optimized CPU and GPU implementations.; Reports 9.17x average energy reduction versus CPU and 1.75x versus GPU.

TL;DR: STEEL maps FlashAttention-style causal prefill attention onto AMD XDNA-like NPUs to reduce energy and latency for long-sequence LLM inference on laptop SoCs.

Problem: The paper studies energy-efficient local inference for LLM-based agents on laptop-class SoCs, focusing on the difficulty of mapping attention mechanisms to NPUs with diverse architectures and explicit data-movement models.

Method: It proposes STEEL, an open-source FlashAttention implementation for XDNA-like NPUs, using a dataflow formulation of prefill attention plus sparsity-aware pipeline placement to handle causal-mask load imbalance across the NPU array.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is the first open-source FlashAttention implementation targeting XDNA-like NPUs, with a causal-mask-aware placement strategy that reduces synchronization overhead and improves utilization.

Main result: On AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370, STEEL reportedly reduces energy by 9.17x versus CPU and 1.75x versus GPU baselines on average; it also reports 9.6x average latency reduction over prior XDNA 1 work and 22.8x average speedup over layer-by-layer attention on XDNA 2.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide code URL, benchmark details, sequence lengths, model sizes, ablations, statistical robustness, or evidence beyond AMD/XDNA-class hardware.

Relevance: Relevance to the matched query 'causality' is weak: the paper discusses causal masks in autoregressive attention, not causality or causal inference. It may still matter if the feed intentionally includes causal attention systems work.

Tags: FlashAttention, NPU, XDNA, LLM inference, energy efficiency, causal mask, systems

Figure note: A useful figure would show the STEEL dataflow for prefill attention on an XDNA NPU array, including sparsity-aware placement for causal-mask-induced load imbalance.

Authors: Victor J. B. Jung, Gagandeep Singh, Joseph Melber, Kristof Denolf, Francesco Conti, Luca Benini

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: DKCD: Domain Knowledge-Enhanced Causal Discovery from Unstructured Data
item.description:
Priority: 38/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 18, Paper 35, Empirical 52, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index data, DOI, journal or conference venue, code availability, data availability

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-10 in cs.CL.; Abstract identifies two challenges: insufficient latent factor identification and unreliable factor annotation.; DKCD has three components: Knowledge Mining, Knowledge-guided Causal Reasoning, and Causal Structure Discovery.; Abstract reports experiments on two domain-specific datasets with significant improvements, but gives no numerical results.

TL;DR: DKCD proposes a domain-knowledge-enhanced framework for extracting latent causal factors from unstructured high-expertise-domain data and using them to improve causal graph construction.

Problem: The paper studies causal discovery from unstructured data in domains such as healthcare, finance, and education, where important causal factors may be implicit and where unreliable annotation can propagate errors into causal graphs.

Method: It introduces DKCD, a three-part pipeline: knowledge mining retrieves relevant domain knowledge from observable factors; knowledge-guided causal reasoning uses that knowledge to infer latent causal factors and causal clues for annotation; causal structure discovery constructs graphs from the expanded factor set and annotations.

Key contribution: The novel claim is the explicit use of retrieved domain knowledge to improve both latent factor discovery and domain-grounded annotation before causal graph construction, rather than relying only on general LLM knowledge.

Main result: The abstract reports experiments on two domain-specific datasets and says DKCD significantly improves causal factor identification and causal graph construction, but it does not provide metric values, dataset names, or baseline details.

Limitation: Evidence is limited in the supplied abstract: no code, data availability, metrics, baseline names, ablations, robustness checks, affiliations, DOI, venue, or citation information are provided.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causal discovery' because the paper directly targets causal discovery from unstructured data and includes causal reasoning and causal graph construction as central components.

Tags: causal discovery, causal reasoning, LLMs, unstructured data, domain knowledge, latent factors, causal graphs

Figure note: A pipeline or architecture figure showing the flow from unstructured data and observable factors through domain knowledge retrieval, latent factor reasoning, annotation, and final causal graph construction would be useful.

Authors: Xin Li, Jin Li, Shoujin Wang, Kun Yu, Fang Chen

Query: causal discovery, causal reasoning, causality

PDF

item.title: WILDTRACE: Benchmarking Natural Evidence Trails in Long-Context Reasoning
item.description:
Priority: 51/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 16, Paper 55, Empirical 45, Reproducibility 30, Manuscript 65

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations, Author citation or h-index information, DOI, journal, or conference venue, Model benchmark results, Baselines, Ablations

Score evidence: Introduces WILDTRACE with 481 tasks over 214 naturally occurring long-form sources.; Defines seven source-internal evidence geometries drawing on Pearl's causal hierarchy and multi-hop reasoning typologies.; Uses a source-first construction pipeline and multi-stage validation for necessity, groundedness, rubric fidelity, contamination resistance, and answerability.; Metadata shows arXiv v1 with no DOI, journal reference, or comment.

TL;DR: WILDTRACE is a new long-context reasoning benchmark designed to test whether models can integrate naturally dispersed, source-internal evidence trails rather than recover planted or distributionally artificial facts.

Problem: The paper studies complex question answering over long documents where the necessary evidence is naturally scattered across distant passages and must be integrated through causal, temporal, or narrative relations.

Method: It introduces WILDTRACE, a benchmark with 481 tasks over 214 naturally occurring long-form sources, and defines seven source-internal evidence geometries informed by Pearl's causal hierarchy and prior multi-hop reasoning typologies. The construction pipeline mines candidate evidence trails from document structure before writing questions, followed by multi-stage validation for clue necessity, groundedness, rubric fidelity, contamination resistance, and answerability.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is a benchmark focused on natural evidence trails that arise from the document's own structure and logic, contrasting with needle probes, planted facts, or reverse-engineered multi-hop chains that may introduce distributional artifacts.

Main result: The abstract describes the benchmark design and validation process but does not report model performance numbers, baselines, or comparative empirical results.

Limitation: No empirical model results, baselines, ablations, code release, data availability details, affiliation signals, or DOI/conference information are provided in the supplied metadata or abstract.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query 'causality' because the benchmark explicitly uses Pearl's causal hierarchy and includes causal evidence integration as a core long-document reasoning demand, though the paper appears primarily about long-context NLP evaluation rather than causal discovery or causal inference methods.

Tags: long-context reasoning, benchmark, question answering, multi-hop reasoning, causal reasoning, evidence integration, NLP evaluation

Figure note: A useful image would be a pipeline or taxonomy figure showing the seven source-internal evidence geometries and the source-first benchmark construction and validation workflow.

Authors: Zixin Chen, Peng Liu, Haobo Li, Rui Sheng, Jianhong Tu, Xiaodong Deng, Fei Huang, Kashun Shum, Dayiheng Liu, Huamin Qu

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Causal Perspectives on Network Meta-Analysis
item.description:
Priority: 61/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 38, Paper 55, Empirical 55, Reproducibility 30, Manuscript 80

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, citation counts or h-index signals, DOI or peer-reviewed venue, code availability, public data availability, simulation design details

Score evidence: arXiv v1 in stat.ME, published 2026-07-10.; Abstract states a unified causal framework for pairwise and network meta-analysis using aggregate data.; Abstract explicitly defines target populations and heterogeneity from treatment-effect modifiers and center effects as central to identification.; Numerical studies are mentioned, including cases where proposed and conventional estimators diverge.

TL;DR: The paper reframes pairwise and network meta-analysis as a causal estimand-first problem, arguing that clinically meaningful target populations and heterogeneity sources imply arm-level aggregation rather than conventional contrast-based aggregation.

Problem: Standard pairwise and network meta-analyses usually aggregate study-level treatment contrasts, but the abstract argues this leaves the causal estimand and target population insufficiently defined, especially under treatment-effect modifiers and center effects.

Method: The authors propose a unified causal framework for aggregate-data pairwise and network meta-analysis that first defines treatment effects for a target population, then derives the aggregation needed for identification; in networks, this leads to arm-level aggregation and identification that does not rely on the treatment network itself.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is conceptual and methodological: arm-level aggregation is derived from a causal identification argument rather than chosen as a modeling preference, providing a causal perspective on the contrast-based versus arm-based network meta-analysis debate.

Main result: The abstract reports numerical studies where the proposed estimators target well-defined causal effects; conventional approaches often give similar estimates but can diverge in some settings, affecting interpretation of meta-analytic evidence.

Limitation: The abstract does not report real-data validation, details of simulation design, baseline methods, robustness checks, code availability, or practical conditions under which divergence becomes clinically important.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because it applies causal estimand-first reasoning, identification, treatment-effect modifiers, and heterogeneity modeling to network meta-analysis.

Tags: causality, network meta-analysis, pairwise meta-analysis, causal estimands, aggregate data, evidence-based medicine, arm-level aggregation, stat.ME

Figure note: A useful figure would diagram the estimand-first causal workflow: target population and heterogeneity sources leading to identification and arm-level aggregation, contrasted with standard contrast-based network meta-analysis.

Authors: Ahmed Boughdiri, Francisco Andrade, Clément Berenfeld, Julie Josse

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Causally Debiased Latent Action Model for Embodied Action Conditioned World Models
item.description:
Priority: 52/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 36, Paper 48, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 76

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index metadata, DOI or venue, code repository, public data or dataset names, baseline identities and implementation details

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-10 in cs.CV and cs.RO.; Abstract introduces metrics for latent-action bias, action following, and robustness.; Abstract reports experiments on 2B and 14B ACWM backbones.; Abstract claims 6k fine-tuning steps and more than 12x fewer robot-action adaptation updates than the baseline.

TL;DR: CD-LAM proposes causal debiasing objectives for latent action models so action-conditioned world models become more controllable and less driven by action-irrelevant visual factors.

Problem: The paper studies how to learn controllable action-conditioned world models for embodied agents when large-scale real-world action-labeled robot video is costly, and when latent action models trained only with reconstruction objectives entangle controllable action dynamics with irrelevant visual context.

Method: It proposes CD-LAM, a causally debiased LAM-based ACWM framework using three fine-tuning objectives: embodiment-centric reconstruction, action-centric contrastive learning, and latent space calibration. The paper also introduces metrics for latent-action bias, action following, and robustness.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is that action-irrelevant bias is identified and measured as a core obstacle for controllable LAM-based ACWMs, then addressed with a targeted causal-debiasing fine-tuning framework rather than reconstruction-only latent action learning.

Main result: From the abstract, experiments on 2B and 14B ACWM backbones report improved latent-action controllability, downstream robot-action following, visual fidelity, and adaptation efficiency, with only 6k fine-tuning steps and over 12x fewer robot-action adaptation updates than the baseline.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide dataset names, baseline details, ablations, statistical robustness, code availability, or failure cases, so the strength and reproducibility of the empirical evidence cannot be fully assessed from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query "causality" because it frames controllability failures in embodied world models as action-irrelevant bias and proposes causal debiasing objectives for latent action representation learning.

Tags: causality, latent action models, world models, robotics, embodied AI, controllability, representation learning, computer vision

Figure note: A useful figure would show the CD-LAM pipeline: unlabeled video to latent action inference, the three fine-tuning objectives, calibration of the latent action space, and evaluation on ACWM controllability/action-following metrics.

Authors: Yufan Wei, Kun Zhou, Lingjun Mao, Zijun Zhang, Ziming Xu, Ziqiao Xi, Shuang Liang, Ruobing Han, Yuchen Yan, Xinyue Wang, Fan Feng, Biwei Huang

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: DominoTree: Conditional Tree-Structured Drafting with Domino for Speculative Decoding
item.description:
Priority: 61/100 (Skim, confidence: high)

Quality dimensions: Author 30, Paper 45, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 70, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index metadata, DOI or peer-reviewed venue, citation or influential-citation data, statistical significance or variance, ablation details for top-M and CUDA builder choices

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-09 in cs.CL.; Abstract reports Qwen3-4B experiments across eight benchmarks and Qwen3-8B follow-up results.; Abstract names baselines: autoregressive decoding, released Domino, DDTree, CaDDTree, and DFlash.; Comment states: 23 pages, 2 figures, 11 tables, and code at https://github.com/slin-zhq/Domino-Tree.

TL;DR: DominoTree is a training-free best-first speculative decoding draft-tree method that scores candidate paths with Domino's conditional, path-dependent correction and reports higher accepted length and throughput than Domino, DDTree, CaDDTree, and DFlash on Qwen3 models.

Problem: The paper studies how to accelerate LLM inference with speculative decoding when draft generation methods either use factorized per-position marginals or cannot exploit Domino's path-dependent token corrections inside tree search.

Method: It proposes DominoTree, a best-first draft-tree construction procedure that scores each root-to-node path using Domino's conditional non-factorized GRU-based correction, while restricting per-node correction to a top-M candidate set for efficiency. It also introduces a GPU-native CUDA-graph tree builder intended to match a Python reference exactly while keeping per-round construction cheap.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element, based on the abstract, is adapting best-first tree drafting to Domino's conditional path-dependent distributions rather than using DDTree-style factorized marginal scores, plus a GPU-native builder that preserves acceptance behavior.

Main result: On Qwen3-4B across eight benchmarks, DominoTree reports up to 6.6x speedup over autoregressive decoding, mean accepted length up to 10.7 tokens per round, 9-10% overall throughput gain over released Domino, up to +22% on Alpaca, and wins over DDTree/CaDDTree at every tested temperature. On Qwen3-8B it keeps the highest accepted length at every temperature and reports +24% throughput over DDTree at T=0, though the high-temperature edge narrows to a tie or small loss.

Limitation: The abstract does not state training cost because the method is training-free, but it also does not provide enough detail on statistical significance, ablations for top-M, robustness across model families beyond Qwen3, hardware dependence, or full reproducibility quality beyond a GitHub link.

Relevance: The configured query is "causality"; this paper is only weakly relevant if the feed is intended for causal inference or causality research, because "causal" here appears to mean autoregressive/path-dependent correction in decoding rather than causal discovery or causal reasoning. It is relevant for LLM inference acceleration and speculative decoding.

Tags: speculative decoding, LLM inference, draft trees, Domino, CUDA graphs, training-free decoding, causal correction, weak causality-query match

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper's framework figure showing how DominoTree expands and scores a best-first draft tree using Domino's path-dependent conditional corrections, ideally contrasting it with DDTree's factorized marginal tree scoring.

Authors: Saw S. Lin, Jyh-Shing Roger Jang

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Native Video-Action Pretraining for Generalizable Robot Control
item.description:
Priority: 40/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 34, Paper 40, Empirical 45, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 60

Missing quality signals: affiliations, citation count, h-index or author impact metadata, DOI, journal or conference acceptance, open-source code

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper in cs.RO and cs.CV, published 2026-07-09.; Abstract names four design principles: semantic visual-action tokenizer, causal pretraining, sparse MoE backbone, and enhanced asynchronous inference.; Abstract claims real-world deployment and few-shot generalization across complex manipulation tasks.; No DOI, journal reference, conference venue, code link, quantitative metrics, or affiliation metadata are supplied.

TL;DR: LingBot-VA 2.0 proposes a native video-action foundation model for robot control, built around causal pretraining, semantic visual-action tokenization, sparse MoE capacity, and asynchronous closed-loop inference.

Problem: The paper studies how to adapt video-action modeling to physical robot control, arguing that video generative models designed for digital content creation are inadequate for embodied, real-time manipulation environments.

Method: It proposes LingBot-VA 2.0, a video-action foundation model with a semantic visual-action tokenizer, scratch-trained causal pretraining, a sparse Mixture-of-Experts backbone, and asynchronous inference that predicts future latents while re-grounding rollouts on the latest robot observations.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim in the abstract is a robotics-native video-action pretraining framework rather than repurposing bidirectional content-generation video models, with causality treated as a core temporal modeling principle for control.

Main result: The abstract reports real-world deployment and few-shot generalization across complex manipulation tasks, but does not provide quantitative metrics, baselines, dataset details, or task counts.

Limitation: Evidence is abstract-level only: no numerical results, baseline comparisons, ablations, robustness tests, code, public data, affiliations, or citation signals are provided in the supplied metadata.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query "causality" because the model explicitly uses a causal pretraining paradigm for temporal robot-control dynamics, though this appears to concern autoregressive/causal sequence modeling rather than causal inference or causal discovery.

Tags: robotics, video-action models, causal pretraining, foundation models, embodied AI, mixture-of-experts, closed-loop control

Figure note: A useful image would be the LingBot-VA 2.0 architecture or pipeline figure showing the semantic visual-action tokenizer, causal pretraining flow, sparse MoE backbone, and asynchronous closed-loop inference loop.

Authors: Qihang Zhang, Lin Li, Luyao Zhang, Shuai Yang, Yiming Luo, Shuaiting Li, Ruilin Wang, Junke Wang, Jiahao Shao, Gangwei Xu, Jiaming Zhou, Yishu Shen, Yudong Jin, Fangyi Xu, Shuailei Ma, Jiaqi Liao, Guanxing Lu, Zifan Shi, Yongkun Wen, Yujie Zhao, Weixuan Tang, Xinyang Wang, Chaojian Li, Jiapeng Zhu, Ka Leong Cheng, Nan Xue, Xing Zhu, Yujun Shen, Yinghao Xu

Query: causality

item.title: CAAD: Causality-Aware Multivariate Time Series Anomaly Detection via Multi-Scale Alignment and Structural Causal Consistency
item.description:
Priority: 63/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 10, Paper 78, Empirical 68, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 82

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations are not supplied., Author citation counts or h-index are not supplied., DOI and journal reference are absent., No code repository or open-source implementation is mentioned., Dataset names, metrics, quantitative results, ablations, and robustness checks are not provided in the abstract., No explicit limitation discussion is visible in the supplied abstract.

Score evidence: Title explicitly targets causality-aware multivariate time-series anomaly detection.; Abstract claims CAAD verifies Granger causality consistency through exogenous variables.; Abstract describes residual modeling, multi-scale alignment, and a gradient-based matrix for causal relationship breakdowns.; Abstract claims extensive experiments on real-world industrial datasets and outperformance over most state-of-the-art baselines.

TL;DR: CAAD proposes causal-consistency monitoring for multivariate industrial time-series anomaly detection, treating anomalies as deviations in Granger-causal structure rather than only temporal representation similarity.

item.title: Certified Interventional Fidelity: Anytime-Valid, Adaptive Evaluation of Causal Claims in Mechanistic Interpretability
item.description:
Priority: 68/100 (Skim, confidence: high)

Quality dimensions: Author 47, Paper 78, Empirical 68, Reproducibility 70, Manuscript 82

Missing quality signals: Author affiliation is not present in the supplied metadata., Citation count and h-index are not present., DOI and journal reference are absent., The abstract does not list detailed baselines, ablations, sample sizes, statistical robustness checks, or metric definitions., Repository quality and exact reproducibility instructions are not visible from the supplied metadata.

Score evidence: Accepted at UAI 2026 according to the comment field.; Code link is provided in the metadata: https://github.com/AsiaeeLab/certified-interventional-fidelity.; The abstract states CIF defines the reported quantity as a causal estimand over stated input and intervention distributions.; The abstract reports experiments on MNIST abstractions and GPT-2 Small IOI circuits.

TL;DR: The paper introduces Certified Interventional Fidelity, a statistical framework for turning mechanistic-interpretability intervention evaluations into certified causal estimands with confidence intervals and anytime-valid confidence sequences.

Problem: Mechanistic interpretability often reports fidelity, activation patching, ablation, or compressed-model comparison results as point estimates, even when evaluations are monitored or adaptively steered, making causal claims hard to distinguish from finite-sample artifacts or evaluation-choice effects.

Method: CIF formalizes the reported interventional quantity as an expectation of a bounded score over explicitly stated input and intervention distributions, then supplies confidence intervals and anytime-valid confidence sequences, including adaptive intervention sampling via bounded mixture importance weighting. The paper instantiates this with Hoeffding-style sequences and variance-adaptive betting sequences.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution, based on the abstract, is adding a certification layer to interventional interpretability evaluations so patching/fidelity claims become statistically valid causal estimands, including under adaptive sampling rather than only fixed, post hoc point estimates.

Main result: In experiments on MNIST abstractions and GPT-2 Small IOI circuits, CIF reportedly certifies high-fidelity claims, identifies apparent method differences that are not statistically supported, exposes sensitivity to the intervention distribution, and variance-adaptive betting sequences reduce certification cost by 10-30x.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide full experimental details, baseline list, ablations, robustness checks, interval widths, sample sizes, or failure cases, so empirical strength and generality beyond MNIST abstractions and GPT-2 Small IOI circuits remain uncertain from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the causality query because it frames interpretability interventions as causal estimands and studies statistically valid evaluation of causal claims under intervention distributions.

Tags: causality, mechanistic-interpretability, interventional-evaluation, confidence-sequences, adaptive-sampling, GPT-2, UAI-2026

Figure note: A useful figure would show the CIF pipeline: interventional evaluation setup, causal estimand definition, adaptive intervention sampling, and conversion of running scores into confidence intervals or anytime-valid confidence sequences.

item.title: TFP: Temporally Conditioned Memory-Fusion Policies for Visuomotor Learning
item.description:
Priority: 61/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 28, Paper 50, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: DOI or journal reference absent., Author affiliations absent., Citation count and h-index absent., Open-source code not mentioned., Public data release not mentioned., Ablation details not visible in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1, published 2026-07-09.; Accepted to the SemRob 2026 Workshop at RSS 2026.; Abstract reports benchmark gains on LIBERO and LIBERO-plus.; Abstract reports MIKASA ShellGameTouch diagnostic success up to 75.0%.

TL;DR: TFP adds an event-sensitive, dynamics-aware memory belief to VLA policies and injects it into the action decoder, improving manipulation success on LIBERO-style benchmarks and showing intervention evidence that the belief affects generated actions.

Problem: Reactive VLA policies can fail in stage-dependent manipulation because visually similar observations may require different actions depending on latent task progress, occlusion, contact/release events, or prior outcomes.

Method: The paper proposes Temporally Conditioned Memory-Fusion Policies, which maintain an episode-local task-progress belief using Liquid Time-Constant dynamics and adaptively modulate the flow-matching action decoder so accumulated temporal context directly shapes action chunks.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is a lightweight memory-action mechanism where compact, event-sensitive belief dynamics are fused into the VLA action decoder rather than used only as passive history context.

Main result: From the abstract: with a 3.3B-parameter model, TFP improves average success from 96.9% to 98.75% on LIBERO and from 91.4% to 93.77% on LIBERO-plus; it reaches up to 75.0% on MIKASA ShellGameTouch. Mechanistic analyses report about 6x larger write-gain changes near manipulation events and hidden-state interventions suggesting causal modulation of action chunks.

Limitation: The abstract does not report code, public data release, statistical uncertainty, ablations, broader baseline coverage, affiliation/citation metadata, or detailed failure analysis. The venue signal is a workshop acceptance rather than a main conference or journal.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query 'causality' because the abstract explicitly reports hidden-state interventions showing the learned belief causally modulates generated action chunks, within a robotics/VLA manipulation setting.

Tags: robotics, visuomotor-learning, vision-language-action, memory, causality, flow-matching, manipulation, LIBERO

Figure note: A useful image would show the TFP pipeline: VLA backbone, Liquid Time-Constant task-progress belief update, event-sensitive write gate, and adaptive modulation into the flow-matching action decoder.

item.description:

TL;DR: The paper proposes a mixed-effects framework for causal DAG structure learning on clustered data, estimating a global acyclic structure while modeling cluster-level graph variations.

Problem: Existing scalable DAG structure learning methods for causal discovery generally assume a homogeneous population, which limits their use on clustered data with patient-, site-, or group-specific effects.

Method: The authors extend fixed- and random-effects ideas from classical mixed models to DAG structure learning, using a differentiable graph-coupling mechanism that keeps the union of fixed- and random-effects graphs acyclic, plus a provably convergent first-order optimization method with batched cluster updates.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element, based on the abstract, is coupling mixed-effects modeling with differentiable acyclic DAG learning so that both global and cluster-level causal structures can be learned without violating acyclicity.

Main result: The abstract reports identifiability, asymptotic recovery of the true structure, and experiments on real and synthetic data where the method detects dependencies missed by alternative estimators; no quantitative metrics are provided in the supplied abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide quantitative results, named baselines, dataset details, code availability, ablations, robustness checks, affiliations, DOI, or publication venue, so empirical strength and reproducibility are uncertain.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causal discovery' because it directly targets scalable DAG structure learning for causal discovery and extends it to clustered or heterogeneous populations.

Tags: causal discovery, DAG structure learning, clustered data, mixed effects models, random effects, differentiable optimization, statistical identifiability

Figure note: A useful figure would show the proposed mixed-effects DAG framework: a shared fixed-effects graph, cluster-specific random-effects graphs, and the differentiable coupling constraint that keeps their union acyclic.

item.title: Workload-Preserving Differentially Private Synthetic Data for Causal Inference via Maximum-Entropy Calibration
item.description:
Priority: 67/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 40, Paper 76, Empirical 65, Reproducibility 70, Manuscript 82

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations are not supplied., Citation counts and h-index signals are not supplied., DOI and journal reference are absent., Dataset names, metric values, and experimental scale are not visible in the abstract., Ablations, statistical robustness, and detailed baseline coverage are not specified in the abstract., Repository quality and data availability are not verifiable from the supplied metadata alone.

Score evidence: Accepted at UAI 2026 according to the comment field.; Code link is supplied: https://github.com/AsiaeeLab/causal-aim.; Abstract states a theory decomposing ATE error into sampling, privacy, workload-approximation, Monte Carlo, and calibration terms.; Abstract reports empirical comparison: causal workloads help most at strict privacy budgets and for calibrated uncertainty, while generic workloads may retain point-RMSE advantage as privacy relaxes.

TL;DR: The paper proposes differentially private synthetic-data workloads tailored to doubly robust causal-estimation moments so that released synthetic data better supports ATE, ATT, and subgroup causal analyses under privacy constraints.

Problem: Workload-based DP synthetic data often optimizes generic distributional fidelity, but causal estimands such as ATE depend on treatment balance and outcome moments that generic marginal workloads may fail to preserve.

Method: The authors introduce causal workloads built around orthogonal moments used by doubly robust causal estimators, with two use modes: direct estimation through stable moment-map estimators or reconstruction into reusable synthetic data via maximum-entropy calibration. They also propose Causal-AIM for adaptive workload selection and NA+MI for noise-aware confidence intervals.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is aligning the private query workload with causal estimating moments rather than generic marginals, plus an error decomposition covering sampling, privacy, workload approximation, Monte Carlo, and calibration terms.

Main result: From the abstract, causal workloads are most useful under strict privacy budgets and for calibrated uncertainty; generic workloads can still outperform on point RMSE when privacy constraints are relaxed.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide dataset names, effect-size numbers, ablation details, robustness checks, or enough evidence to judge when Causal-AIM consistently beats generic workloads for point estimation.

Relevance: This directly matches the configured query 'causal inference' because it studies how to preserve causal estimands and uncertainty validity when releasing DP synthetic data.

Tags: causal inference, differential privacy, synthetic data, average treatment effect, doubly robust estimation, maximum-entropy calibration, uncertainty quantification

Figure note: A useful figure would show the proposed pipeline from DP causal workload selection through noisy query release, moment-map estimation or maximum-entropy calibration, and NA+MI uncertainty estimation.

item.title: SAGA: Stable Acceleration Guidance for Autoregressive Video Generation
item.description:
Priority: 44/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 14, Paper 40, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index metadata, DOI or venue signal, open-source code, public data details, full baseline list

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper in cs.CV, published 2026-07-09.; No DOI, journal reference, or comment is supplied.; Training-free method applicable to existing chunk-wise autoregressive diffusion models.; Reports Self-Forcing Temporal Quality 97.30 to 97.91 and Image Quality 69.60 to 70.51.

TL;DR: SAGA is a training-free guidance method that uses latent acceleration spectra and structured autoregressive noise initialization to reduce temporal instability in chunk-wise autoregressive video diffusion.

Problem: The paper studies error amplification in autoregressive video diffusion, where generated latents reused as causal context can produce flickering, motion jitter, and structural drift over long horizons.

Method: It proposes Stable Acceleration Guidance (SAGA), combining an acceleration-domain spectral guidance objective based on finite-window Slepian projections with structured autoregressive noise initialization to suppress short-range temporal correlations while preserving long-range motion.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element, from the abstract, is diagnosing temporal failure through discrete latent acceleration and using acceleration-domain spectral guidance without retraining or modifying the diffusion backbone.

Main result: Across multiple autoregressive diffusion models, SAGA reportedly improves temporal quality. On Self-Forcing, Temporal Quality improves from 97.30 to 97.91 and Image Quality from 69.60 to 70.51; spectral analysis and human preference studies are also claimed.

Limitation: The abstract does not report code, datasets, full baseline comparisons, ablations, statistical significance, compute cost, or robustness across diverse video domains; gains on the named metric appear modest.

Relevance: The match to the configured query 'causality' is indirect but real: the method targets autoregressive video generation where generated latents serve as causal context, and it studies instability caused by causal reuse over time.

Tags: autoregressive video generation, video diffusion, temporal stability, spectral guidance, latent acceleration, training-free guidance, cs.CV

Figure note: A useful figure would show the SAGA pipeline: autoregressive latent chunks, finite-window acceleration computation, Slepian spectral projection guidance, and structured noise initialization before each generated chunk.

item.title: Can We Trust LLM's Logic? Quantifying Uncertainty, Coherence, and Robustness via a Graph-Based Framework
item.description:
Priority: 5/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 3, Paper 5, Empirical 6, Reproducibility 3, Manuscript 7

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations are not present in supplied metadata., Citation count and h-index are not present., No DOI, journal, or conference venue is listed., Code and data availability are not mentioned., Dataset names, model names, exact metrics, effect sizes, and statistical tests are not included in the abstract., Baseline coverage beyond Self-Consistency is unclear from the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1, published 2026-07-09.; Metadata lists categories cs.CL and cs.AI.; Comment reports 42 pages, 14 figures, and 12 tables.; Abstract introduces GRAPHEVAL, GRCS, GSC, and adversarial medoid ablation.

TL;DR: GRAPHEVAL reframes LLM reasoning uncertainty as graph-based reasoning fidelity, using coherence metrics and medoid-based decoding to detect and prefer more faithful reasoning paths beyond final-answer agreement.

Problem: The paper studies how to quantify uncertainty and trustworthiness in LLM reasoning when standard decoding methods such as Self-Consistency can agree on final answers while ignoring invalid or unfaithful intermediate reasoning.

Method: It proposes GRAPHEVAL, a graph-based framework over reasoning traces, plus the Graph Reasoning Coherence Score (GRCS) for semantic-structural consensus, Graph Self-Consistency (GSC) as a medoid-based decoding strategy, and adversarial medoid ablation to test robustness of selected reasoning paths.

Key contribution: The novel element is treating uncertainty quantification as holistic reasoning-fidelity evaluation rather than answer-level voting, with graph topology used to expose mode collapse, confident hallucinations, and load-bearing reasoning paths.

Main result: From the abstract: GRCS is reported as the only metric consistently negatively correlated with reasoning faithfulness across both stronger and smaller models; GSC trades nominal accuracy for fidelity in smaller models while preserving or improving accuracy in stronger models; adversarial removal of the GSC-selected path degrades faithfulness and sometimes accuracy.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide dataset names, exact effect sizes, model list, baseline set beyond Self-Consistency, ablations beyond medoid ablation, code/data availability, affiliation signal, or statistical robustness details. The stated accuracy-fidelity tradeoff also suggests possible cost for smaller models.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query 'causality' because the paper explicitly asks whether semantic, structural, and causal awareness can select more faithful reasoning, and it studies robustness under adversarial interventions on reasoning topology. However, it appears primarily about LLM reasoning evaluation/UQ rather than causal inference methods directly.

Tags: LLM reasoning, uncertainty quantification, graph evaluation, self-consistency, reasoning faithfulness, causal awareness

Figure note: A framework figure showing reasoning traces as a graph, GRCS computation, GSC medoid selection, and adversarial medoid ablation would be useful; no direct image URL is provided in the metadata.

TL;DR: The paper studies how coding agents change code review and proposes a grey-literature, LLM-assisted theory-building method that yields a causal model of how team expertise and review process choices moderate AI's impact.

Problem: Coding agents increasingly author pull requests, but existing repository-mining studies capture unstable surface trends and do not explain whether AI makes review a bottleneck, reduces the need for human review, or erodes reviewer understanding.

Method: The authors combine a motivating observational analysis of public GitHub pull requests with large-scale synthesis of practitioner discourse: 38,709 grey-literature documents are collected, filtered for code-review relevance, and a stratified random sample of 3,100 documents is coded using an LLM-assisted pipeline to construct a causal model.

Key contribution: The main novelty is an explicit causal theory of AI-era code review with 26 constructs and 67 relationships, plus a reusable LLM-assisted grey-literature theory-building pipeline for software-engineering research.

Main result: From the abstract, agent-authored pull requests appear to be reviewed less often, merged several times faster, and discussed less than human-authored pull requests, but these trends reverse under defensible alternative analyses; the stronger contribution is the resulting causal model rather than a stable empirical effect estimate.

Limitation: The abstract reports no validation against controlled studies, no ablations for the LLM-assisted coding pipeline, no inter-rater reliability details, and no robustness evidence beyond noting instability in repository-mining trends.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the causality query because it turns conflicting observations about AI-authored pull requests into falsifiable causal propositions with named constructs, moderators, and directed or contested relationships.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide model names, benchmark names, quantitative metrics, baselines, ablations, statistical robustness, code availability, or enough implementation detail to judge reproducibility.

TL;DR: This paper analyzes why post hoc linearized causal self-attention loses quality and proposes frozen-backbone state-update and cache-routing interventions that improve long-context transformer inference across LLaMA and Qwen models.

Problem: Quadratic causal self-attention is a bottleneck for long-context transformer inference, and existing post hoc linearization pipelines make it hard to identify which design choices preserve model quality.

Method: The paper studies transformer linearization in a strict frozen-backbone regime, analyzes softmax attention as relying on key-dependent rank-1 orthogonal projections, and introduces structural interventions including sink tokens, short convolutions, and fixed-budget cache routing.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim from the abstract is an analysis-driven explanation of why delta-style state updates outperform purely gated accumulation, followed by targeted interventions to reduce approximation error in post hoc linearized transformers without retraining the backbone.

Main result: The abstract reports scaling the approach across LLaMA and Qwen models up to 32B parameters, outperforming prior post hoc baselines on MMLU and matching the long-context retrieval performance of more complex adaptive-caching frameworks.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide exact benchmark numbers, datasets beyond MMLU and retrieval tasks, ablations, robustness tests, code availability, or details on memory/latency tradeoffs.

item.description:

TL;DR: STRACE proposes a causal trace-selection and localization framework to turn noisy long-horizon agent execution traces into higher-signal contexts for LLM-based agent optimization.

Problem: The paper studies how to optimize long-horizon agents from execution traces when trace collections are redundant and heterogeneous, and individual trajectories contain irrelevant steps that can mislead reflection-based LLM optimizers.

Method: STRACE operates at two levels: it mines batch-level failure patterns to filter redundant traces and keep representative failures, then performs causal localization within selected traces using a textual dependency graph to remove non-causal steps and identify the root-cause module to optimize.

Key contribution: The main novelty is combining structural trace filtering with causal extraction, rather than using naive truncation, sliding windows, or unstructured reflection over raw trajectories.

Main result: The abstract reports significant gains over standard context-filtering baselines, including a VeruSAGE-Bench improvement from 42.5% to 58.5% success rate for human-expert designed agents, described as a 1.4x improvement.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide details on datasets beyond VeruSAGE-Bench, ablations, statistical robustness, failure cases, or how the textual dependency graph is constructed and validated.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the configured query 'causality' because it uses causal localization and root-cause extraction over agent trajectories to improve LLM-based optimization.

item.title: Institutional Red-Teaming: Deployment Rules, Not Just Models, Causally Shape Multi-Agent AI Safety
item.description:
Priority: 55/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 36, Paper 42, Empirical 76, Reproducibility 38, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: author affiliation, author citation count or h-index, DOI, journal or conference venue, open-source code, public dataset or benchmark repository

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-08 with no DOI, journal reference, or comment in the supplied metadata.; Benchmark claims 228 contexts, five canonical rules, seven model populations, and 33,924 games.; Abstract reports a controlled rule-varying setup intended to attribute behavior changes to deployment rules.; Abstract reports an anonymization ablation on gpt-5.1 and repeated-play follow-up.

TL;DR: The paper proposes an institutional red-teaming methodology showing that deployment rules can causally change collective safety outcomes in multi-agent AI systems, even when agents, objectives, and task states are held fixed.

Problem: It studies how deployment rules, rather than only model capabilities or prompts, shape safety outcomes in multi-agent AI settings and whether changing a single rule can be causally attributed to changes in collective behavior.

Method: The authors introduce institutional red-teaming: keep agents, objectives, and task state fixed, vary one deployment rule, and attribute behavioral changes to that rule. They instantiate this in IABench-CA across 228 contexts, five consequence-allocation rules, seven model populations, 33,924 games, a normative cooperative reference, and auto-labelled reasoning traces.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is shifting red-teaming from testing models alone to testing institutional deployment rules as causal interventions, packaged as a safety-case workflow that certifies provisional rule regions per context and population.

Main result: From the abstract, changing only the consequence rule moves mean fatality by 22 to 58 percentage points within every population; identity-targeting is never decisively safest, eliminates the least-resourced agent in 30-87% of games, and an anonymization ablation raises targeted elimination from 22% to 81% when the loss bearer is named.

item.title: Graph-Regularized Deep Learning for EEG-Based Emotion Recognition with Psychologically-Grounded Label Structure
item.description:
Priority: 45/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: high)

Quality dimensions: Author 24, Paper 42, Empirical 62, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 58

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index data, DOI, venue, or journal reference, open-source code link, full baseline list, ablation results

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-08 with no DOI or journal reference in supplied metadata.; Evaluated on SEED-IV and SEED-V datasets.; Reports three backbone architectures: AudioTransformer, Conformer, and DCGNN.; Reports best-case +5.42% accuracy and 39% reduction in psychologically implausible misclassifications.

TL;DR: The paper proposes graph-based regularizers that encode psychological relationships among emotion labels to improve EEG emotion recognition across multiple neural backbones.

Problem: EEG-based emotion recognition models commonly treat emotion classes as independent labels, which can ignore psychologically meaningful relationships between emotions and lead to implausible misclassifications.

Method: It models emotion classes as nodes in a graph with edges derived from dimensional emotion theories, then regularizes predictions using Graph Label Smoothing, Graph Laplacian commuting distance, and Sliced Wasserstein Distance across AudioTransformer, Conformer, and DCGNN backbones.

Key contribution: The main novelty is injecting a psychologically grounded emotion-label topology into otherwise standard EEG deep learning classifiers through architecture-agnostic graph regularization, rather than changing only the backbone architecture.

Main result: On SEED-IV and SEED-V, the abstract reports consistent gains, with a best-case accuracy improvement of up to 5.42% and a 39% reduction in psychologically implausible misclassifications.

Limitation: The abstract does not report statistical significance, ablations, full baseline details, robustness tests, implementation details, or released code; it only states that code will be released.

Relevance: The paper matched the configured query 'causality' mainly through its use of DCGNN as one evaluated backbone and graph-structured label dependencies, but the abstract does not indicate that the proposed method studies causal inference directly.

Tags: EEG, emotion recognition, graph regularization, label structure, graph Laplacian, optimal transport, deep learning, causality-adjacent

Figure note: A useful figure would show the emotion graph topology and how the three regularizers act on model predictions across the EEG backbone outputs.

item.title: FourierQK: Spectral Preprocessing of Query-Key Projections Improves Transformer Attention
item.description:
Priority: 43/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 24, Paper 35, Empirical 62, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 58

Missing quality signals: author affiliation, author citation or h-index metadata, peer-reviewed venue, DOI or journal reference, open-source code, repository quality

Score evidence: arXiv v1, published 2026-07-08; no DOI or journal reference in supplied metadata.; Abstract reports TinyShakespeare validation losses for fixed random, single-frequency, and four-frequency spectral filters.; Single-frequency result is reported across three random seeds with mean val=0.236 and std=0.019.; Abstract includes controls against random Q/K projections and a shuffled-validation diagnostic against positional leakage.

TL;DR: FourierQK proposes FFT-based spectral preprocessing of transformer Q/K projections and reports large TinyShakespeare character-level language-modeling gains, while finding that causal spectral filters do not help under this tokenization.

Problem: The paper studies whether globally mixing learned query/key projections in the frequency domain can improve transformer attention, and where the approach breaks down under causal attention constraints.

Method: It applies spectral preprocessing to Q/K projections before standard dot-product attention, testing fixed random spectral filters, learned single-frequency filters, learned multi-frequency filters, random projection controls, shuffled-validation diagnostics, and causal filter variants such as Gaussian, Mexican Hat, and Morlet.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is preserving the usual attention score structure while modifying only Q/K through FFT-based bilateral spectral preprocessing, distinguishing it from FNet-style Fourier mixing of token embeddings and identifying a boundary between bilateral non-causal spectral attention and causal spectral attention.

Main result: On TinyShakespeare, the abstract reports validation loss improving from standard attention by Delta=+1.166 with four learned frequencies, reaching val=0.309 and described as a 79% reduction; a single-frequency setup is reported across three seeds with mean val=0.236 and std=0.019.

Limitation: Evidence in the abstract is limited mainly to TinyShakespeare character-level modeling; no code, broader datasets, full hyperparameter details, peer review, DOI, or external validation are provided in the supplied metadata. The method is explicitly non-causal in its bilateral FFT form and causal filters did not improve over standard attention at character-level tokenization.

Relevance: The paper matched the query "causality" because it directly discusses causal versus non-causal spectral attention: the reported gains come from bilateral FFT kernels that couple positions to future tokens, while causal spectral filters fail in this setting.

Tags: transformers, attention, spectral-methods, Fourier, query-key-projections, causality, language-modeling

Figure note: The metadata says the paper has 2 figures, but no direct image URL is supplied. A useful image would be the FourierQK architecture or pipeline figure showing FFT-based preprocessing applied specifically to Q/K before standard attention.

item.title: The Blind Curator: How a Biased Judge Silently Disables Skill Retirement in Self-Evolving Agents
item.description:
Priority: 50/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 20, Paper 40, Empirical 68, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 62

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-08 in cs.AI, cs.CL, and cs.CR.; Abstract claims a corrupted-reward analysis and controlled corruption injection into deterministic reward.; Experiments are described on a reference-free report-writing testbed with a code-generation cross-check.; Abstract reports that false-pass bias disables contribution-based retirement past a sharp threshold.

TL;DR: The paper analyzes how false-pass bias in LLM judges can silently disable contribution-based skill retirement in self-evolving agents, even when aggregate evaluation metrics may not reveal the failure.

Problem: It studies self-evolving agents whose growing skill libraries rely on reward-based retirement of bad skills, focusing on the case where reference-free tasks require biased LLM judges that may fail to observe or correctly score failures.

Method: The abstract describes a corrupted-reward analysis plus behavioral experiments that inject controlled corruption into a deterministic reward, evaluated on a reference-free report-writing testbed with a code-generation cross-check.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is that judge bias, especially false-pass bias, does not merely add noise but can disable the skill-retirement curator past a sharp threshold, producing a mechanism-level safety failure that may be invisible in aggregate performance metrics.

Main result: Symmetric noise reportedly leaves retirement intact, while false-pass bias disables contribution-based retirement beyond a threshold that additional data cannot overcome; the mechanism is said to hold across domains and failure rates except for near-zero-false-pass verifier-like graders.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide quantitative metrics, dataset details, baseline comparisons, ablations, statistical tests, code availability, or enough implementation detail to assess reproducibility or empirical strength.

Relevance: The paper matched the query "causality" because it explicitly frames the analysis as isolating the causal channel of reward corruption and studying a mechanism by which biased judges disable skill retirement.

Tags: self-evolving agents, LLM-as-judge, reward corruption, causal analysis, agent safety, skill retirement, reference-free evaluation

Figure note: A useful figure would show the self-evolving-agent pipeline with skill synthesis, judge reward, contribution-based retirement, and the false-pass corruption path that disables the curator.

item.title: DeLS-Spec: Decoupled Long-Short Contexts for Parallel Speculative Drafting
item.description:
Priority: 55/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 24, Paper 55, Empirical 65, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: Affiliations not supplied., Citation count and h-index not supplied., DOI, journal, or conference venue not supplied., No code or data availability signal in metadata., No numeric empirical results in abstract., No ablations, statistical robustness, or detailed baseline list beyond DFlash in abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-08 in cs.CL.; Motivates a specific gap: DFlash position-wise block predictions lack explicit intra-block causal conditioning.; Positions Domino and DSpark as causal alternatives that require training from scratch.; Proposes a fixed DFlash long-context expert plus independently trained lightweight local short-context head.

TL;DR: DeLS-Spec improves block-parallel speculative decoding by adding an independently trained lightweight short-context head to a fixed DFlash long-context drafter, aiming to recover intra-block causal conditioning without retraining the draft model from scratch.

Problem: The paper studies how to make block-parallel speculative drafting for LLM inference faster and more causally conditioned within generated token blocks, while avoiding the cost and inflexibility of training draft models from scratch.

Method: It proposes DeLS-Spec, a decoupled long-short context speculative decoding framework that treats a fixed DFlash model as a long-context expert and adds a lightweight local head as a short-context expert trained independently with standard next-token prediction. At inference, it combines long-context and short-context logits.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is modular causal enhancement for block-parallel drafting: intra-block short-context information is introduced through a detachable local head rather than joint training or full draft-model retraining as in methods such as Domino and DSpark.

Main result: The abstract reports experiments on Qwen3 models where DeLS-Spec consistently improves speedup and average acceptance length over DFlash across math, code, and dialogue benchmarks, but gives no numeric gains.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide quantitative speedup values, acceptance-length numbers, ablations, robustness analysis, implementation details, code availability, or comparisons beyond DFlash; claims about lower training cost are qualitative in the supplied metadata.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the configured query `causality` because it targets missing intra-block causal conditioning in block-parallel speculative decoding and proposes a lightweight way to add short-context causal information.

Tags: speculative decoding, LLM inference, parallel drafting, causality, DFlash, Qwen3, efficient inference

Figure note: A useful figure would show the DeLS-Spec inference pipeline: fixed DFlash long-context expert, independently trained local short-context head, logit combination, and verifier acceptance path.

item.description:

Quality dimensions: Author 16, Paper 35, Empirical 60, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 65

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-08.; Matched query is 'causality'.; Abstract states an error-only transient elastodynamics baseline with similar mean error fails a stricter causality check.; Abstract reports static elasticity and transient elastodynamics numerical examples.

TL;DR: PA-SciML proposes a verification-first workflow for LLM-agentic scientific machine learning that audits surrogate models against explicit physics requirements, including causality, instead of selecting only by prediction error.

Problem: The paper studies how agentic SciML systems can select surrogate models that achieve low error while still violating mechanics-relevant physical properties such as boundary conditions, superposition, stiffness scaling, or causality.

Method: It introduces Physics-Audited Agentic SciML, a workflow that fixes the scoring evaluator before search, derives reviewable machine-checkable physics requirements, checks each trained candidate on predicted outputs, and searches input/load-history ranges for high-violation cases even without reference solution fields.

Key contribution: The genuinely new emphasis is per-candidate physics evidence on predicted fields within an agentic model-discovery loop, separating verification of stated physical requirements from aggregate error-based scoring.

Main result: In static elasticity examples, PA-SciML selects a surrogate with lower validation error than an error-only baseline while both pass common linear-elastic checks; in transient elastodynamics, an error-only baseline with similar mean error fails a stricter causality check, while the selected surrogate passes.

Limitation: The abstract reports only computational solid-mechanics numerical examples and does not provide code, public data, affiliations, DOI, peer-review status, ablations, statistical robustness, or broader benchmark evidence.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the keyword 'causality' because it evaluates whether transient surrogate models improperly respond to future loading history and uses causality as a physics-audit requirement.

Tags: agentic SciML, LLM agents, surrogate modeling, physics verification, causality, computational mechanics, elastodynamics

Figure note: A useful figure would show the PA-SciML pipeline: fixed evaluator, physics-requirement derivation, agentic surrogate search, per-candidate output audits, violation search over input/load-history spans, and verified reporting.
