Problem: The paper studies the lack of standardized data for rare, high-risk and long-tail autonomous-driving safety scenarios, especially datasets that combine trajectories with risk labels, semantic annotations, and verifiable safety signals.

Method: It proposes K-Risk, a dataset and risk-centric extraction pipeline that integrates 20 human-driven and autonomous-vehicle trajectory datasets from Europe, China, and the United States, extracts high-risk events, and augments them with LLM-generated language triplets: structured scenario descriptions, abnormal-behavior notifications, and for a representative subset, causal risk analyses plus action recommendations validated in a closed-loop simulator with iterative reflection.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element, according to the abstract, is the combination of multi-source structured driving trajectories, risk-centric event extraction, LLM-generated semantic supervision, causal risk analysis, and simulator-validated decision recommendations in one standardized high-risk driving dataset.

Main result: The abstract reports that K-Risk contains 31,398 high-risk events and a 1,036-event extreme near-collision subset, covering highways, urban freeways, intersections, and roundabouts across Europe, China, and the United States.

Limitation: The abstract does not report benchmark results, model performance gains, annotation quality metrics, inter-rater validation, baselines, ablations, code availability, or details on how simulator validation was quantified.

Problem: LLM sycophancy is usually treated as one behavior, but the abstract argues it may consist of distinct factual and opinion-based forms with potentially different internal mechanisms.

Method: The authors dissociate sycophancy into factual and opinion subtypes, train linear probes on activations for one subtype, construct steering vectors, and evaluate transfer to the other subtype as evidence about shared versus distinct representations.

Key contribution: A representation-dissociation framework for complex LLM behaviors that tests whether behavioral subtypes share mechanisms or causally interfere when steered across subtypes.

Main result: The abstract reports evidence that different LLMs encode factual and opinion sycophancy differently: some appear more unified, while others show more distinct and causally interfering representations.

item.title: Geometric Collapse: When Vision Models Fail to Verify Physical Causality
item.description:
Priority: 60/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 32, Paper 70, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index metadata, DOI, journal reference, confirmed venue status beyond metadata comment, open-source code

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-08.; Metadata comment says ICML 2026, but DOI and journal reference are absent.; Abstract reports experiments on NYU Depth v2 and KITTI across CNN, ViT, SSL, diffusion, and flow-matching depth estimators.; Abstract reports up to 3.2x larger deviation than energy-matched noise and only 47% recovery under oracle-mask output repair.

TL;DR: The paper introduces Scrambled Edges, a controlled counterfactual test showing that modern depth predictors can globally collapse when given physically unsupported edge cues.

Problem: It studies whether large-scale self-supervised and dense geometric vision models perform inference-time physical plausibility checks, especially when edge-like evidence violates surface continuity, illumination coherence, and occlusion ordering.

Method: The authors propose Scrambled Edges, a counterfactual corruption that injects salient edge-like cues while using energy-matched and structure-matched controls to separate unsupported edge evidence from generic high-frequency noise or edge sparsity.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is a controlled behavioral probe for physical-causality failures in dense prediction: it tests whether models can quarantine implausible local cues rather than merely measuring robustness to standard noise or corruptions.

Main result: Across CNN, ViT, and self-supervised depth predictors on NYU Depth v2 and KITTI, Scrambled Edges cause up to 3.2x larger deviation from clean predictions than energy-matched noise; diffusion and flow-matching depth estimators degrade less but still show significant collapse. Output-level repair with oracle mask knowledge recovers only 47%, with errors propagating outside the corrupted region.

Limitation: The abstract does not report full metric tables, statistical tests, exact model list, code/data availability, ablations beyond the named controls, or whether the ICML 2026 claim is accepted, under review, or workshop-related.

Relevance: This is relevant to the matched query 'causality' because it directly frames dense vision failures as failures to verify physical causality and uses controlled counterfactual interventions to test causal plausibility in model predictions.

Tags: computer-vision, depth-estimation, causality, counterfactual-evaluation, robustness, physical-plausibility

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper's framework or pipeline figure showing clean input, Scrambled Edges construction, matched controls, depth-predictor response, and global Geometric Collapse propagation.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper in cs.CL, published 2026-07-07.; Evaluates six instruction-tuned LLMs spanning 14B to 70B parameters.; Introduces conditional Dialect Group Invariance and feature-level localization analysis.; Reports negative concord as a universal trigger across all evaluated models.

TL;DR: The paper audits and mitigates LLM bias that rewrites African American English into Standard American English, proposing cDGI auditing, feature-level bias localization, and activation steering as a training-free mitigation.

Problem: Instruction-tuned LLMs may misinterpret or normalize African American English by preferring Standard American English continuations even when the input context is in AAE.

Method: The authors evaluate six instruction-tuned LLMs from 14B to 70B, introduce conditional Dialect Group Invariance to separate model bias from translation artifacts, localize AAE features that trigger bias, and apply causal-tracing-derived activation steering directions at test time to reduce dialect bias.

Key contribution: The genuinely new elements claimed in the abstract are cDGI for dialect-bias auditing, feature-level localization of AAE markers that trigger bias, the first application of activation steering to dialect bias, and the REAL-AAE parallel corpus of 17,479 AAE/SAE/AAE_back tweet-derived triplets.

Main result: Across all six models, syntactic constructions, especially negative concord such as "ain't nobody", are universal bias triggers; activation steering reportedly reduces bias 5 to 20 times more than prompting while preserving SAE fluency.

Limitation: The abstract does not report full benchmark details, statistical uncertainty, ablations, robustness tests, code availability, model names, or whether results generalize beyond tweet-derived AAE data.

Relevance: Matched the configured query "causality" because the mitigation extracts dialect directions via causal tracing and uses activation steering, making it relevant to causal representation analysis in LLM behavior even though the main topic is dialect bias.

Tags: LLM bias, African American English, activation steering, causal tracing, dialect robustness, NLP fairness, cs.CL

item.title: Embracing Spillover: Spatial Effects in Experiments
item.description:
Priority: 54/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 14, Paper 50, Empirical 30, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 80

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index evidence, DOI or venue signal, conference or journal acceptance, citation count, open-source code

Score evidence: Abstract states that spillover effects violate independent-unit assumptions in spatial experiments.; Defines causal estimands as linear functionals of a spillover kernel.; Claims kernel shape is identifiable but level is not under cluster randomization with fixed exposure set size.; Provides exact bias decomposition and a scalar level-leverage diagnostic.

TL;DR: The paper develops a causal-inference framework for spatial experiments with spillover, showing that common estimands depend on a spillover kernel whose shape can be identified under certain cluster-randomized designs but whose level requires an anchoring assumption or design augmentation.

Problem: The paper studies causal estimation in spatial experiments where interventions affect nearby or otherwise spatially related units, creating interference that invalidates standard no-spillover trial analyses.

Method: It formulates direct, indirect, total, and dose-response effects as linear functionals of a spillover kernel under cluster randomization with fixed exposure set size, then analyzes identification, bias, anchoring assumptions, and design augmentations within that framework.

Key contribution: The main novelty is the explicit identification result that the kernel shape is identifiable but its level is aliased with the intercept and direct effect, plus an exact pre-data bias decomposition and a scalar 'level leverage' diagnostic for estimand sensitivity to the unidentified level.

Main result: From the abstract, the main result is theoretical: any anchored estimator's bias decomposes exactly into level bias, kernel-shape misspecification error, and geometry-driven leakage, and conventional cluster-trial analysis emerges as a special implicit-anchor case with closed-form contamination bias.

Limitation: The abstract does not report empirical validation, simulations, real-data examples, code, or robustness checks; evidence appears primarily theoretical from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query 'causality' because it addresses causal estimands, interference, spillover, identification, and bias in spatial experiments, a focused causal-inference setting.

Tags: causal inference, spatial experiments, interference, spillover, cluster randomization, identification, bias decomposition, experimental design

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework diagram showing the spillover kernel, anchored level, bias decomposition terms, and how design augmentations recover the unidentified level.

item.title: Prompt-Adapter Context Routing for Parameter-Efficient Multi-Shot Long Video Extrapolation
item.description:
Priority: 54/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-07 in cs.CV and cs.AI.; Abstract claims a frozen text-to-video diffusion transformer augmented with low-rank temporal adapters.; Uses recursive prompt bank storing entity, location, action, and style prompts from previous shots.; Reports comparison against several baseline families across six multi-shot and long-video benchmarks.

TL;DR: PACR-Video proposes lightweight prompt-routed temporal adapters for frozen text-to-video diffusion transformers to improve coherent multi-shot long video extrapolation.

Problem: The paper studies long-horizon multi-shot video extrapolation, where generated future shots must preserve recurring entities, scene structure, visual style, and causal/narrative progression without expensive full generator fine-tuning.

Method: PACR-Video freezes a text-to-video diffusion transformer and adds low-rank temporal adapters conditioned on learned shot-role prompt tokens. It builds a recursive prompt bank of entity, location, action, and style prompts from previous shots, routes them through adapter gates based on predicted narrative dependencies, and trains with a Shot-Local/Story-Global objective combining next-shot reconstruction, cross-shot identity contrast, and prompt sparsity regularization.

Key contribution: The main novelty claimed in the abstract is combining compact recursive prompt memory, dependency-aware prompt routing, and low-rank temporal adapters to provide controllable long-video capacity without full model fine-tuning.

Main result: The abstract reports that PACR-Video outperforms text-to-video, tuning-based, memory-augmented, streaming, and recursive-context baselines across six multi-shot/long-video benchmarks on quality, semantic alignment, identity consistency, temporal smoothness, motion stability, transition coherence, and human preference; exact numbers are not provided.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide quantitative effect sizes, benchmark names, architecture details, code/data availability, ablations, robustness tests, or evidence that the causal progression claim reflects formal causal modeling rather than narrative coherence.

Relevance: Relevant to the query 'causality' mainly through causal progression and predicted narrative dependencies in video extrapolation, but it appears closer to long-video generation/coherence than to causal inference or causal discovery.

Tags: text-to-video, long video generation, diffusion transformers, parameter-efficient tuning, prompt routing, temporal adapters, narrative coherence, causal progression

Figure note: The comment indicates 2 figures, but no direct image URL is supplied. A useful image would be the PACR-Video pipeline showing the frozen diffusion transformer, recursive prompt bank, adapter gates, and shot-local/story-global training flow.

item.title: Final Checkpoints Are Not Enough: Analyzing Latent Reasoning Faithfulness Along Training Trajectories
item.description:
Priority: 54/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 34, Paper 42, Empirical 62, Reproducibility 30, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations and prior topic record not supplied., Citation count, h-index, and influential-citation signals not supplied., No DOI, venue, journal, or conference acceptance signal supplied., Datasets, model sizes, latent reasoning paradigms, metrics, baselines, ablations, and statistical robustness are not specified in the abstract., No code repository, public data link, or implementation details supplied.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 published 2026-07-07; no DOI, journal reference, or comment supplied.; Abstract explicitly studies latent reasoning faithfulness across saved training checkpoints.; Method includes verifiable counterfactual input edits and noise-ablation activation patching.; Reported findings distinguish output-level and activation-level trajectories and note answer-format dependence.

TL;DR: This paper studies how the causal faithfulness of latent reasoning steps changes during training, showing that final converged checkpoints can hide divergent training-time trajectories.

Problem: Latent reasoning methods perform multi-step inference in hidden states, but it is unclear whether those hidden-state reasoning steps faithfully and causally determine final answers, especially as training progresses.

Method: The authors track saved checkpoints across training for different latent reasoning paradigms, using verifiable counterfactual input edits and noise-ablation activation patching on latent reasoning steps.

Key contribution: The novel angle is temporal: instead of evaluating only converged checkpoints, the paper analyzes faithfulness trajectories during training and connects output-level counterfactual behavior with activation-level causal contribution.

Main result: From the abstract, converged latent reasoning methods can appear similarly unfaithful under counterfactual edits while having different training trajectories; activation-level contribution of latent reasoning decays across training for both paradigms, and the trajectory depends on answer format, decaying for binary choice but rising for open-ended decoding.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify datasets, model families, exact latent reasoning paradigms, baselines, ablations beyond the stated interventions, statistical robustness, code release, or whether results generalize beyond the tested settings.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the causality query because it uses causal interventions and activation patching to test whether latent reasoning states causally drive model outputs.

Tags: latent reasoning, faithfulness, causal intervention, activation patching, training dynamics, counterfactual evaluation, cs.LG, cs.CL

Figure note: A useful figure would plot faithfulness over training checkpoints, with separate curves for output-level counterfactual flip behavior and activation-level causal contribution, split by latent reasoning paradigm and answer format.

item.title: Direct and efficient estimation of bilinear forms in staggered tensor panels
item.description:

Score evidence: Abstract explicitly links the structured missingness pattern to staggered adoption designs common in causal inference.; Proposes a spectral algorithm that pools across tensor layers and estimates the functional directly.; Claims non-asymptotic MSE bounds and local minimax lower bounds matching up to constants.; Reports experiments on simulated and real-world datasets, but without abstract-level details.

TL;DR: The paper studies direct estimation of bilinear forms from noisy, partially observed Tucker2 tensor panels with staggered-adoption missingness, proposing a pooled spectral estimator with non-asymptotic and minimax guarantees.

Problem: Estimating a target bilinear functional from noisy tensor data when observations are incomplete in structured patterns motivated by staggered adoption designs, without needing full tensor completion.

Method: The authors analyze a four-block missingness pattern, propose a spectral algorithm that pools information across tensor layers and targets the bilinear form directly, then extend it to general staggered adoption designs through an anchored four-block reduction.

Key contribution: The main novelty is a direct functional-estimation approach for staggered tensor panels, with theory showing a phase transition in the number of layers where pooling becomes beneficial, plus matching local minimax lower bounds up to constants.

Main result: The abstract reports non-asymptotic mean squared error bounds, local minimax lower bounds matching up to constants, analogous guarantees for general staggered adoption, and validation on simulated and real-world datasets; no numerical results are provided in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify empirical baselines, datasets, metrics, code availability, robustness checks, or practical assumptions required by the Tucker2 and missingness models.

Relevance: Relevant to the causal inference feed because the missingness structure is explicitly motivated by staggered adoption designs, a common setup in causal inference, although the paper is primarily statistical theory for tensor panel estimation rather than an applied causal identification paper.

Tags: causal inference, staggered adoption, tensor panels, Tucker2 model, bilinear forms, spectral algorithm, non-asymptotic theory, minimax lower bound

Figure note: A useful image would be the framework or pipeline figure showing the four-block missingness pattern, anchored reduction for staggered adoption, and how the spectral estimator pools layers to estimate the bilinear form directly.

item.title: Cross-Trajectory Chimera Interventions Reveal Dissociable Roles of Weight Magnitude and Direction in Grokking
item.description:
Priority: 50/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 26, Paper 42, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 78

Missing quality signals: author affiliation absent, author citation count or h-index absent, DOI absent, journal or conference venue absent, open-source code not indicated, public data not indicated

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-07.; Abstract reports experiments on two modular-arithmetic tasks that grok.; Cross-trajectory chimera intervention recombines weight-vector norm and unit direction across two seeds.; Direction transfer reached donor circuit identity in 40/40 cases; random angle-matched control showed no shift.

TL;DR: The paper proposes cross-trajectory chimera interventions to test which partially trained neural-network weight properties causally transfer across independently seeded grokking runs, finding that weight direction transfers circuit identity while norm mainly controls overwrite susceptibility.

Problem: It studies whether causal properties identified within one training trajectory are portable across independently trained networks, focusing on grokking in modular-arithmetic tasks.

Method: The method splits each run's weight vector into norm and unit direction, recombines one run's norm with another run's direction, resumes training, and compares the resulting circuit identity against controls and threshold-localization experiments.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is a cross-trajectory causal intervention framework showing a dissociation between weight direction and magnitude: direction carries transferable donor-specific solution identity, while norm predicts when that identity can overwrite the recipient trajectory.

Main result: From the abstract: implanting a donor direction at the recipient norm drove the run to the donor's circuit in 40/40 cases, angle-matched random controls caused no shift, and threshold locations separated perfectly by norm class over 20 pairs with joint permutation probability 1.9e-4.

Limitation: Evidence in the abstract is limited to two modular-arithmetic grokking tasks; no code, public data, broader architectures, robustness tests, affiliations, DOI, peer-review venue, or independent replication evidence are provided in the supplied metadata.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the causality feed because it introduces causal interventions for neural-network training trajectories and tests cross-run portability rather than single-run necessity.

Tags: causality, grokking, mechanistic interpretability, training dynamics, cross-trajectory interventions, weight geometry

Figure note: A useful image would be the framework or pipeline figure showing donor and recipient trajectories, norm-direction recombination, resumed training, and the threshold-like transfer result; no direct image URL is available from the supplied metadata.

item.title: MobileWan: Closing the Quality Gap for Mobile Video Diffusion
item.description:
Priority: 62/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 56, Paper 55, Empirical 68, Reproducibility 45, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: Explicit author affiliations, Citation count or h-index metadata, DOI, journal, or conference venue, Open-source code or license statement, Exact device and hardware configuration, Baseline comparison details

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-07 in cs.CV.; Abstract starts from Wan2.2-5B and claims deployment of a 5B-scale video diffusion model on a commercial mobile device.; Reported result: 5-second 480x832 videos at 16 FPS in 20 seconds latency and VBench score 83.79.; Project page is listed, but the abstract does not state code, data, or detailed benchmark setup.

TL;DR: MobileWan reformulates and compresses a 5B video diffusion transformer so it can run high-quality 5-second video generation on commercial mobile hardware with constant-memory, causal/autoregressive inference.

Problem: The paper studies how to close the quality gap between server-scale video diffusion models and mobile video generation systems, where prior mobile models use much smaller parameter budgets and therefore have limited fidelity and motion coherence.

Method: Starting from Wan2.2-5B, the authors use recurrence distillation to convert generation into chunk-wise autoregressive inference with constant-memory attention, combine it with causal linear attention, add learnable binary-gated attention-head pruning with sparsity and distillation losses, and use sampling-step distillation plus memory-optimized VAE decoding.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is that a server-scale 5B video diffusion transformer can be deployed on memory-constrained mobile hardware by making inference RNN-like and combining structured compression, pruning, step distillation, and optimized decoding, instead of designing a much smaller mobile-only model.

Main result: The abstract reports 5-second 480x832 videos at 16 FPS generated in 20 seconds end-to-end latency on a commercial mobile device, with a VBench score of 83.79 and a claimed new state of the art in mobile video generation.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify the exact mobile device, baselines, ablations, robustness tests, human evaluation, code availability, or whether the reported latency and VBench results generalize across prompts and hardware.

Relevance: The match to the configured query "causality" appears to come from causal linear attention and autoregressive chunk-wise generation, not from causal inference or causal modeling; it is relevant if the feed tracks causal/causal-attention mechanisms in generative architectures, but likely peripheral for causal reasoning research.

Tags: video diffusion, mobile inference, causal linear attention, recurrent distillation, model compression, attention head pruning, edge AI

Figure note: A useful image would be the MobileWan system pipeline showing recurrence distillation, chunk-wise causal/RNN-style inference, attention-head pruning, sampling-step distillation, and mobile VAE decoding.

TL;DR: The paper probes whether vision-language models contain causally manipulable reward-valuation mechanisms that resemble human anhedonia-related reward circuitry.

Problem: It studies whether alignment between VLMs and human cognition extends to reward valuation and whether specific internal units causally affect effort-reward decision behavior in an anhedonia-like way.

Method: The authors build a mechanistic evaluation framework inspired by clinical tests for anhedonia and motivational deficits, identify reward-anticipatory units in VLMs, and apply targeted perturbations to test causal effects on reward-based choices.

Key contribution: The novel claim is that VLMs contain functionally identifiable reward-anticipatory units whose perturbation selectively shifts behavior toward low-effort, low-reward choices, paralleling clinical anhedonia rather than merely degrading general task capability.

Main result: From the abstract, perturbing NAc-selective units induces anhedonia-like behavior in effort-based decision tasks while preserving baseline performance when reward-based choice is removed; the induced vulnerability also aligns with DARS and MAP-SR clinical scales.

Limitation: The abstract does not name the VLMs, datasets, perturbation details, baselines, ablations, statistical tests, code availability, or robustness checks, so the strength and generality of the causal claim are unclear from supplied metadata alone.

item.title: Fingerprint, Not Blueprint: How Positional Schemes Set the Default Spectral Algebra of Attention
item.description:
Priority: 68/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 38, Paper 55, Empirical 82, Reproducibility 78, Manuscript 83

Missing quality signals: author affiliation, citation count, h-index, DOI, journal or conference acceptance, full baseline list

Score evidence: arXiv v1, 16 pages, 5 figures, 4 tables.; Code and data URL is provided in the comment metadata.; Empirical scope includes seven pretrained models, three positional schemes, and public Pythia checkpoints.; Abstract reports exact permutation p=0.029 and four pre-registered contrasts with q_BH <= 0.016.

TL;DR: The paper argues that positional encoding schemes shape the default spectral fingerprints of attention-head QK operators, but these spectra are consolidated after circuit function emerges rather than serving as necessary causal blueprints.

Problem: It studies what the non-Hermitian spectrum of the attention pre-softmax bilinear operator M = W_q^T W_k encodes for previous-token and induction circuits, especially under different positional schemes.

Method: The paper combines spectral analysis of QK operators across seven pretrained models, developmental tracking over public Pythia checkpoints, causal interventions such as zeroing RoPE phase components, and constrained toy-model training experiments that ban specific spectral channels.

Key contribution: Its main novelty is framing attention-head QK matrices as non-normal operators whose spectra reveal positional-scheme-specific circuit fingerprints, then testing whether those fingerprints are causal requirements or post-formation signatures.

Main result: Across seven pretrained models and three positional schemes, strong previous-token heads separate perfectly by spectral rotational structure at every examined top-k, with exact permutation p=0.029; zeroing the per-frequency RoPE phase eliminates induction on a pre-identified previous-token head in all three RoPE models. Toy experiments suggest no spectral channel is strictly necessary, though bans delay circuit formation with q_BH <= 0.016.

Limitation: Evidence is limited to the examined model set, public Pythia checkpoints, and toy-scale constrained training; the abstract does not report broader task coverage, full baseline details, ablations beyond the described contrasts, or external peer review.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the query 'causality' because it separates spectral fingerprints from causal necessity using interventions and constrained training, while focusing on transformer attention circuits.

Tags: transformer-interpretability, attention-heads, positional-encodings, spectral-analysis, non-Hermitian, causal-interventions, induction-heads, RoPE

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper's framework or pipeline figure showing how QK spectral features, positional schemes, circuit emergence, and causal interventions are connected.

item.title: Learning Sparsest Linear Causal DAGs with Latent Confounders via Higher-Order Cumulants
item.description:
Priority: 46/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 40, Paper 45, Empirical 58, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 65

Score evidence: arXiv v1 published 2026-07-07 in cs.LG.; Abstract states the method recovers the sparsest DAG in LvLiNGAM using higher-order cumulants.; Abstract claims no restriction on the number of latent confounders.; Abstract reports simulation studies and real-data analyses with superior finite-sample performance.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a finite-sample method using higher-order cumulants to recover the unique sparsest DAG in linear non-Gaussian acyclic causal models with latent confounders.

Problem: Recovering the exact causal DAG in LvLiNGAM with latent confounders is difficult because the model is identifiable only up to an observational equivalence class, and finite-sample recovery of the unique sparsest DAG remains challenging.

Method: The abstract describes a finite-sample recovery method based on higher-order cumulants for learning the sparsest linear causal DAG, without restricting the number of latent confounders.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is an explicit finite-sample procedure for recovering the unique sparsest DAG in LvLiNGAM while allowing an arbitrary number of latent confounders, unlike prior asymptotically consistent methods with stronger restrictions.

Main result: The abstract reports that simulations and real-data analyses show superior finite-sample performance compared with existing approaches, but it does not provide datasets, metrics, effect sizes, or statistical details.

item.title: Estimating Causal Effects from Data Generated by Stochastic Algorithms
item.description:
Priority: 60/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Score evidence: arXiv v1, 49 pages, categories stat.ME and econ.EM.; Abstract targets recommendation systems and chatbots using stochastic algorithms.; Method requires logging an unexposed item and a probability ratio for each user.; Abstract claims identification even with unobserved confounders affecting preferences and item-pair identity.

TL;DR: The paper proposes an observational-data method for identifying causal effects of content features in recommendation systems and chatbots driven by stochastic exposure algorithms.

Problem: It studies how to estimate whether content characteristics causally affect outcomes such as engagement when exposed content is tailored to users and varies along many dimensions, making standard observational comparisons confounded.

Method: The proposed method uses logged stochastic exposure data plus, for each user, an unexposed candidate item and an estimated probability ratio comparing how likely the unexposed item was to be shown versus the exposed item.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is an identification and estimator strategy that can recover feature-level causal effects even with unobserved confounders affecting user preferences and the considered exposed/unexposed item pair, using data elements not required by standard approaches.

Main result: The abstract states an identification result for causal effects under the proposed logging setup, but does not report empirical experiments, numerical estimates, datasets, or benchmark comparisons.

Limitation: Evidence in the supplied abstract is mainly methodological; empirical validation, practical logging burden, sensitivity to probability-ratio estimation error, baselines, ablations, and robustness checks are not described.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query "causality" because it directly addresses causal effect identification and estimation under stochastic algorithmic assignment in recommender and chatbot settings.

Tags: causal inference, stochastic algorithms, recommendation systems, chatbots, observational data, content features, identification, stat.ME

Figure note: A useful image would be a pipeline or causal diagram showing stochastic exposure, exposed and unexposed candidate items, logged probability ratio, content features, unobserved confounding, and outcome estimation.

item.title: Controlling Tool Use with Heading-Specific Activation Steering
item.description:
Priority: 48/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 24, Paper 45, Empirical 65, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 75

Missing quality signals: No affiliation metadata supplied., No citation or h-index metadata supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference signal supplied., No code or repository signal supplied., No public data details supplied., No baselines, ablations, statistical robustness, or metric details in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1, published 2026-07-07.; Evaluated across five open-source models and three domains.; Reports bidirectional causal control over tool-invocation behavior.; Finds diffuse, bimodal alignment rather than clean linear structure.

TL;DR: The paper studies whether LLM tool-invocation behavior can be causally controlled via activation steering and finds that heading-specific steering vectors can suppress or induce tool use despite lacking clean linear internal geometry.

Problem: Tool-augmented LLMs often call external tools unnecessarily; the paper asks whether the decision to invoke a tool has a stable internal representation that can be extracted and manipulated at inference time.

Method: The authors extract steering vectors from heading-anchor positions and test whether these vectors causally control tool-invocation behavior across five open-source models and three domains, followed by geometric analysis of alignment patterns and cross-tool feature overlap.

Key contribution: It reports bidirectional causal control over tool use while arguing that the underlying representation is not a simple linear encoding: tool-invocation activations show diffuse, bimodal alignment and tool-type-specific internal signatures.

Main result: From the abstract: steering vectors suppress unnecessary tool use most effectively in domains where parametric reasoning is sufficient, across five open-source models and three domains.

Limitation: The abstract leaves open why geometrically irregular representations still yield causal control, and does not specify datasets, metrics, baselines, ablations, code availability, or robustness tests.

Relevance: The paper matches the configured query 'causality' because it explicitly studies bidirectional causal control of LLM tool-use behavior through activation interventions.

Tags: activation steering, tool-augmented LLMs, causal control, mechanistic interpretability, tool use, representation geometry

Figure note: A useful image would be a pipeline or framework figure showing heading-anchor extraction, steering-vector construction, intervention during generation, and the geometric alignment analysis across tool types.

item.title: ARMS: Anchor-Relational Motion Streaming for Seamless Solo-Social Motion Transitions
item.description:

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper in cs.CV, published 2026-07-07.; Comment states: Accepted by ECCV 2026.; Project page is listed: https://hkliu.com/arms.; Abstract claims improved transition smoothness and social coherence versus interaction-centric baselines.

TL;DR: ARMS proposes a causal streaming diffusion framework for generating long-horizon human motion that can move smoothly between solo behavior and human-human interaction from text.

Problem: The paper studies text-conditioned human motion generation in realistic temporal streams where people may act alone, enter social interaction, and later disengage, a setting where fixed-length clip generators with static agent configurations are brittle.

Method: It introduces an Anchor-Relational Motion Streaming framework with a dynamics-asymmetric representation, partner-referenced relative translation, causal latent-space generation, segment-by-segment causal relational diffusion, and mode-aware relational gating for switching cross-agent connections on or off.

Key contribution: The main novelty is unifying solo and interaction motion in one causal generative process while explicitly modeling solo-social transitions and long-horizon spatial consistency, rather than assuming a fixed number of interacting agents throughout a clip.

Main result: The abstract reports improved transition smoothness and social coherence over interaction-centric baselines, plus competitive performance on human-human interaction benchmarks, but does not provide numeric metrics.

Limitation: The abstract does not report datasets, exact baselines, quantitative gains, ablations, robustness tests, code availability, or failure modes, so empirical strength and reproducibility are uncertain from the supplied metadata alone.

Relevance: The paper matched the query "causality" because its motion generator is explicitly causal: it refines motion segment by segment using only past context for incremental long-horizon generation.

Tags: text-to-motion, human motion generation, human-human interaction, causal diffusion, streaming generation, computer vision, ECCV 2026

Figure note: A framework diagram showing the anchor-relational representation, causal latent segments, relational diffusion refinement, and mode-aware gating between solo and interaction phases would be most useful.

item.title: Memory in the Loop: In-Process Retrieval as ExtendedWorking Memory for Language Agents
item.description:
Priority: 47/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 32, Paper 38, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 62

Missing quality signals: author affiliations not supplied, citation count and h-index not supplied, DOI, venue, and journal reference absent, open-source code not mentioned, public datasets or benchmark definitions not specified, full baseline set and ablations unclear from abstract

Score evidence: arXiv v1; no DOI, journal, or conference reference supplied.; Reports a latency intervention holding a fixed per-turn memory-latency budget and varying store answer speed.; Reports exact permutation p=0.0079 for redundant actions rising with latency.; Reports bounded-window recall improving from 0/5 to 3.6-4.8/5 with in-loop memory across four GPT-5-class models.

TL;DR: The paper argues and tests that moving language-agent memory retrieval into every reasoning step becomes practical when the memory store is in-process rather than networked, reducing latency enough to improve bounded-window recall and reduce redundant actions.

Problem: Language agents typically keep memory outside the observe-reason-act loop, querying it at most once per turn because networked retrieval latency makes per-step memory access expensive.

Method: The paper proposes an in-process retrieval store used inside the agent loop, read and written on every step, and evaluates latency-controlled conditions, bounded-window recall tasks, redundant-action rates, and store-operation timings across GPT-5-class models.

Key contribution: Its main novelty is reframing in-loop memory as a placement/latency problem rather than a retrieval-rationing problem: if memory access is microsecond-scale and constantly available, the authors treat it as extended working memory rather than an external tool.

Main result: From the abstract: redundant actions increased monotonically with memory latency, from 0.0/12 at in-process speed to 7.2/12 at a 110 ms cloud round trip, with exact permutation p=0.0079; in-loop memory improved recall from 0/5 to 3.6-4.8/5 across four GPT-5-class models under a bounded window, with store ops at p50 80-165 us.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide code, public data, full task details, affiliation/citation context, robustness checks beyond the reported latency manipulation, or enough information to judge whether results generalize beyond the tested agent tasks and model family.

Relevance: Relevant to the 'causality' feed because the paper explicitly tests a causal claim about memory latency causing redundant agent actions, though it is primarily an AI agent systems paper rather than a causal inference methods paper.

Tags: language-agents, agent-memory, retrieval, latency, in-process-storage, working-memory, causal-intervention, cs.AI

Figure note: A useful figure would show the proposed observe-reason-act loop with memory read/write inside each step, contrasted with standard once-per-turn external retrieval and annotated with networked versus in-process latency.

Authors: Yusuf Khan, Carlo Lipizzi

item.title: Sensitivity and Early Detection of Bayesian Causal Impact Models for Marketing Interventions
item.description:
Priority: 44/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 38, Paper 35, Empirical 58, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 68

Missing quality signals: author affiliation, author citation or h-index evidence, DOI or venue signal, open-source code, public dataset, baseline methods beyond the two alarm criteria

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper in stat.ME, published 2026-07-06.; Abstract uses daily traffic data from an abandoned cart marketing journey.; Evaluates controlled perturbations across effect magnitudes, confidence levels, and detection horizons.; Compares proportion-based lower-bound alarms with persistence-based negative cumulative-impact alarms.

TL;DR: The paper proposes a simulation framework for using Bayesian Causal Impact models as early-warning monitors for marketing performance degradation rather than only retrospective intervention estimators.

Problem: Marketing interventions and operational changes can degrade performance, but the abstract argues that Bayesian Causal Impact models have not been well studied as tools for early operational monitoring and alarm sensitivity assessment.

Method: The paper repeatedly perturbs observed daily traffic outcomes from an abandoned-cart marketing journey, then evaluates alarm activation probabilities across effect sizes, confidence levels, and monitoring horizons using two criteria: predictive-bound violations and consecutive days of negative cumulative impact.

Key contribution: It reframes Bayesian Causal Impact analysis as a sensitivity-testing and early-detection framework for monitoring decisions, comparing alarm rules under controlled degradations rather than only estimating post-hoc causal effects.

Main result: According to the abstract, detection depends strongly on effect size, confidence level, and horizon; proportion-based predictive-bound alarms weaken as horizons grow, while persistence-based cumulative-impact alarms are more stable and operationally meaningful.

Limitation: The abstract reports one marketing journey data setting and does not mention external datasets, baselines beyond the two alarm criteria, code, statistical uncertainty over simulations, or validation in live monitoring.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the 'causality' feed because it extends Bayesian causal impact modeling to causal monitoring and sensitivity analysis for interventions.

Tags: causal impact, Bayesian methods, early detection, marketing analytics, simulation framework, monitoring

Figure note: A useful figure would show the simulation pipeline: observed marketing time series, controlled degradation perturbations, Bayesian Causal Impact prediction, alarm-rule evaluation, and detection-probability curves across horizons and effect sizes.

item.title: ResonatorLM: Causal Resonant Field Mixing for Efficient Long-Context Language Modelin
item.description:
Priority: 52/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 33, Paper 62, Empirical 58, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 62

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper in cs.CL and cs.AI, published 2026-07-06.; Comment states: 8 pages, accepted at ICANN 2026.; Proposes ResonatorLM as an attention replacement using causal functions of damped resonators.; Reports 6.47x decode speed versus an optimized transformer at 32K tokens in a 6M matched setting.

TL;DR: ResonatorLM proposes a causal, physics-inspired replacement for attention using damped resonator functions to improve efficiency on long-context language modeling.

Problem: The paper studies the efficiency limitations of transformers, RNNs, and CNNs when processing long contexts in language modeling.

Method: It introduces ResonatorLM, which treats token sequences as a driven one-dimensional latent field and replaces attention dot products with causal functions of damped resonators, implemented inside a traditional network architecture.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is replacing self-attention with a causal resonant field-mixing mechanism derived from damped resonator dynamics, aimed at improving long-context training, prefill, and decode efficiency.

Main result: In a small 6M-parameter matched setting, the abstract reports that speedups increase with sequence length, decode speed reaches 6.47x versus an optimized transformer at 32K tokens, and WikiText accuracy reaches 61.31% versus 55.32%.

Limitation: The abstract only reports a small matched setting and limited visible benchmark detail; it does not mention code, ablations, robustness tests, broader model scales, memory results, or enough dataset coverage to judge generality.

Relevance: The paper matches the query 'causality' because its central mechanism uses causal resonator functions for sequence mixing, and it is relevant if the feed tracks causal architectures or alternatives to attention for long-context language models.

Tags: long-context language modeling, attention alternatives, causal sequence modeling, efficient inference, physics-inspired neural networks, resonator dynamics

Figure note: A useful image would show the ResonatorLM architecture or pipeline: token sequence mapped to a one-dimensional latent field, damped resonator causal mixing, and comparison with transformer attention over long contexts.

item.title: From Graphs to Gradients: Physics-Inspired Structural Attribution for Cyber-Physical IoT Systems and Beyond
item.description:
Priority: 57/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 38, Paper 45, Empirical 65, Reproducibility 35, Manuscript 72

Missing quality signals: Author affiliations absent from supplied metadata., Citation counts and h-index signals absent., DOI, journal, conference, or peer-review status absent., No code repository or implementation details provided in supplied metadata., No public data or testbed details provided in the abstract., No numeric metrics, baseline names, ablations, or statistical robustness evidence in the abstract.

Score evidence: Introduces a statistical-mechanics-inspired undirected energy-based representation for cyber-physical IoT systems.; Targets cases where directed causal structure recovery is impractical due to scale, feedback loops, and partial observability.; Reports simulation experiments on an industrial IoT testbed with hybrid continuous and discrete variables.; Claims better attribution accuracy, robustness, and scalability than graph-based approaches.

TL;DR: The paper proposes an energy-based, physics-inspired attribution framework for causal-style explanations in cyber-physical IoT systems where recovering an explicit directed causal graph is impractical.

Problem: It studies how to provide dependency-aware causal explanations for large-scale hybrid cyber-physical systems with feedback loops, partial observability, and mixed continuous/discrete variables, where standard directed causal-graph recovery may not be feasible.

Method: The proposed framework models system-variable dependencies with an undirected energy-based representation inspired by statistical mechanics, then attributes abnormal behavior by analyzing how component perturbations alter the energy landscape.

Key contribution: Its main novelty is shifting causal explanation from explicit directed causal-graph recovery to gradient/energy-landscape-based structural attribution, while still aiming to answer perturbation and influence questions in hybrid IoT systems.

Main result: The abstract reports simulations on an industrial IoT testbed showing higher attribution accuracy, improved robustness, and better scalability than state-of-the-art graph-based approaches, but gives no quantitative metrics.

Limitation: The method is not intended to recover full generative dynamics. The abstract does not provide numeric results, dataset details, baseline names, ablations, statistical tests, code, DOI, venue, or affiliation/citation evidence.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query "causality" because it addresses causal explanation and attribution under interventions, specifically proposing an alternative to causal graph recovery for complex cyber-physical systems.

Tags: causality, causal explanation, structural attribution, energy-based models, cyber-physical systems, industrial IoT, explainable AI

Figure note: A useful image would show the proposed pipeline: hybrid IoT variables mapped into an undirected energy-based dependency model, perturbations changing the energy landscape, and attribution scores compared with graph-based causal explanations.

item.title: GEM-Occ: From Visual Geometry Evidence to Embodied Semantic Occupancy Memory
item.description:
Priority: 49/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 24, Paper 45, Empirical 65, Reproducibility 45, Manuscript 70

Missing quality signals: Affiliations are not supplied., Citation count and h-index signals are not supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference venue is supplied., No quantitative metrics are present in the abstract., No ablations or statistical robustness evidence are described in the abstract., No explicit code repository or released benchmark files are stated in the supplied metadata.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-06 in cs.RO and cs.CV.; Abstract introduces HIOcc using ScanNet, ScanNet++, and Matterport3D.; Abstract describes three evaluation regimes: local prediction, room-level online mapping, and building-level mapping.; Abstract claims experiments compare against prior indoor occupancy and Gaussian-based mapping baselines.

TL;DR: GEM-Occ introduces a hierarchical indoor semantic occupancy benchmark and a Gaussian evidence memory framework for long-horizon embodied mapping across rooms and connected building-scale spaces.

Problem: The paper studies semantic occupancy mapping for embodied indoor agents, especially the gap between existing single-view or room-level occupancy methods and long-horizon semantic mapping across connected indoor environments.

Method: It introduces HIOcc, a benchmark that standardizes ScanNet, ScanNet++, and Matterport3D into a sparse semantic occupancy format, and proposes GEM-Occ, which converts transient local visual geometry predictions into semantic Gaussian occupancy evidence plus free-space ray evidence, then fuses them through visibility- and uncertainty-aware causal updates into local caches, room submaps, and a building-level graph.

Key contribution: The genuinely new element is the combination of a hierarchical multi-dataset benchmark for local, room-level, and building-level occupancy evaluation with a persistent Gaussian evidence memory that treats pointmaps as transient observations rather than the map state itself.

Main result: The abstract reports that GEM-Occ improves local occupancy prediction, online map stability, free-space reasoning, revisit consistency, and building-level scalability over prior indoor occupancy and Gaussian-based mapping baselines, but it does not provide quantitative results.

Limitation: The supplied abstract does not include numerical metrics, ablation details, robustness tests, code availability, data release details, affiliations, citation signals, or concrete failure modes.

Relevance: The paper matched the query "causality" because GEM-Occ uses visibility- and uncertainty-aware causal updates for persistent semantic occupancy memory; however, the relevance is mostly to causal state updating in embodied mapping rather than causal inference or causal discovery.

Tags: semantic occupancy, embodied AI, robotics, computer vision, Gaussian mapping, indoor mapping, benchmark, causal updates

Figure note: A pipeline figure showing transient visual geometry evidence being converted into Gaussian occupancy/free-space evidence and fused into local, room-level, and building-level memory would be useful; the metadata mentions figures and a project page but provides no direct image URL.

item.title: Faithfulness to Refusal: A Causal Audit of Neuron Selectors
item.description:
Priority: 55/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 18, Paper 42, Empirical 75, Reproducibility 30, Manuscript 68

Missing quality signals: author affiliations, author citation or h-index signals, DOI, journal, or conference venue, citation count or influential-citation signal, open-source code, public data or benchmark names

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-06 with no DOI, journal reference, or conference signal in supplied metadata.; Abstract reports audits across five LLMs.; Abstract names activation and magnitude-based baselines plus layer-matched random controls.; Abstract reports behavior outcomes: refusal on hate and crime, benign over-refusal, and language-model fluency.

TL;DR: The paper proposes direct causal audits for neuron-selector faithfulness in LLM refusal behavior, showing that attribution-based selectors can identify causally effective neuron rows while stability-based proxies can be misleading.

Problem: Attribution scores are widely used to choose neuron rows for pruning, interpretability, and safety editing, but the abstract argues that these selectors are rarely tested for whether the chosen rows are actually causally important.

Method: The paper uses two paired audits based on one-shot neuron-row zeroing: a language-modeling audit for identifying dispensable rows, and a behavior-level audit driven by a contrastive harmful-versus-benign signal to test whether selected rows can induce refusal behavior.

Key contribution: It reframes neuron-selector evaluation as a direct causal intervention test and reports that highly rank-stable selectors can be causally weak, while refusal behavior can be installed through multiple largely disjoint sufficient row sets rather than a single unique mechanism.

Main result: From the abstract: attribution methods outperform activation and magnitude baselines at finding dispensable rows across five LLMs; contrastively attributed rows are sufficient to install refusal on hate and crime prompts with low benign over-refusal and preserved fluency, while layer-matched random controls fail.

Limitation: The abstract does not mention code release, dataset details, statistical uncertainty, affiliations, external validation, or whether the audits cover behaviors beyond hate and crime refusal.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the query 'causality' because it tests whether attribution-selected neuron rows are causally responsible for language-model behavior rather than merely correlated or rank-stable.

Tags: causality, LLM interpretability, neuron attribution, refusal behavior, model editing, safety, causal intervention

Figure note: A useful figure would show the paired audit pipeline: selector scoring, neuron-row zeroing intervention, language-modeling audit, contrastive harmful-versus-benign refusal audit, and comparison against random or magnitude/activation baselines.

item.description:

TL;DR: The paper argues that 7-8B open-weight LLMs linearly encode an approximate estimate of remaining output length in their hidden states, including before generation begins.

Problem: It studies whether autoregressive LLMs maintain an internal estimate of how much response remains despite generating text one token at a time.

Method: The authors train minimal-capacity linear probes on frozen hidden states from three open-weight 7-8B models across seven completion-style datasets, then test prompt-state decodability, cross-dataset transfer, synthetic-completion transfer, and behavior around curated retraction/restart events.

Key contribution: The novelty is evidence for a broadly transferable, linearly decodable remaining-length signal, separated from exact counting claims and framed as a plan-like representation rather than a proven causal control mechanism.

Main result: The abstract reports that total response length is linearly decodable from the prompt's final hidden state before output, natural-language-trained probe directions transfer broadly and outperform a statistical baseline, and probe estimates rise during qualitative retraction/restart examples.

Limitation: The abstract does not report aggregate results for the retraction analysis, causal interventions, full metric values, ablations, code, public data, affiliations, citation signals, or peer-reviewed venue information.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query 'causality' mainly because it explicitly distinguishes decodable internal representations from causal use; however, it appears more about mechanistic probing and planning representations than causal intervention or causal inference.

item.title: Causal-RetiGraph: Cross-Cohort Retinal Support and Same-Subject Pathway Analysis for Diabetic Retinopathy
item.description:

TL;DR: Causal-RetiGraph links retinal image-derived graph phenotypes with NHANES pathway modeling to connect diabetic retinopathy lesion evidence, vascular biomarkers, and systemic disease pathways.

Problem: The paper studies how to move beyond accurate diabetic retinopathy image grading toward interpretable connections among local retinal lesions, retinal vascular structure, and systemic microvascular disease pathways.

Method: It proposes Causal-RetiGraph, a two-fold framework: a retinal-image fold builds an interpretable X1234 phenotype from vessel maps, lesion evidence, image embeddings, and AutoMorph biomarkers through spatial X12 and Jacobian X34 branches; an NHANES fold models systemic exposures, covariates, same-subject retinal mediator variables R*, and downstream outcome families.

Key contribution: The main novelty claimed in the abstract is the separation of image-derived retinal support via X1234 from participant-level same-subject pathway summaries via R*, enabling retinal graph phenotypes to prioritize systemic DR pathways without conflating image evidence with mediation.

Main result: The retinal fold reports 0.9055 binary DR accuracy, 0.9711 AUROC, and 0.8312 quadratic weighted kappa for graded DR. NHANES analysis identifies HbA1c, urine albumin, pulse pressure, fasting glucose, and systolic blood pressure as the strongest binary DR anchors, with glycaemic-renal and glycaemic-haemodynamic pathways as the clearest mediator-style signals.

Limitation: The abstract does not report dataset sizes, external validation details, exact baselines, ablations, statistical uncertainty, robustness checks, code availability, or affiliations. Causal strength is also unclear from the abstract because the wording emphasizes pathway prioritization and mediator-style signals rather than confirmed causal effects.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the configured query "causality" because it frames diabetic retinopathy analysis around causal/pathway modeling, mediator-style signals, and cross-cohort links between image phenotypes and systemic exposures.

item.title: Geometric Causal Models
item.description:

Missing quality signals: Affiliations not supplied., Citation count and h-index not supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference reference supplied., No code repository or public data release mentioned., No quantitative empirical results in the abstract., No baselines, ablations, uncertainty calibration, or robustness checks mentioned.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 published 2026-07-06 in stat.ML, cs.LG, and q-bio.BM.; Abstract explicitly proposes geometric causal models for dependent structured data.; Identification is tied to symmetries formalized with group theory and ergodic theory for amenable groups.; Estimation combines geometric deep learning with scalable Bayesian inference.

TL;DR: The paper proposes geometric causal models, a group-theoretic framework for causal inference in dependent structured data such as spatial, graph, and DNA sequence data.

Problem: It studies how to identify and estimate causal effects when observations are structured and dependent rather than i.i.d., including spatial, network, molecular, and DNA-like data.

Method: The framework models symmetries of the data-generating process using group theory, uses ergodic theory for amenable groups for identification, and combines geometric deep learning with scalable Bayesian inference for estimation.

item.title: UNIVERSE: Unified Video Action Models for Autonomous Driving with Flexible Mask-Modulated Modality Generation
item.description:

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-06; metadata lists 18 pages, 7 figures, and 8 tables.; Abstract reports 91.0 PDMS on NAVSIM versus 89.6 for a Two-DiT variant.; Abstract reports zero-shot transfer to nuScenes and Bench2Drive without fine-tuning, but does not give the exact transfer metrics.; Abstract reports ablations for single-DiT unification, video co-training, and mask-based modality decoupling.

TL;DR: UNIVERSE proposes a single mask-modulated Diffusion Transformer that jointly learns future video latents and ego-trajectory tokens so video-based world dynamics supervision transfers more directly to autonomous-driving trajectory generation.

Problem: The paper studies how to transfer the benefits of future video prediction and temporal-causal scene modeling into trajectory planning for autonomous driving, especially under cross-domain generalization.

Method: It introduces UNIVERSE, a unified video-action model with shared generative parameters for future video latents and ego-trajectory tokens, plus a Modality-Decoupling Visibility Mask that shares historical context while blocking attention between future video targets and trajectory targets.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is replacing cascaded or dual-DiT video/action designs with a single DiT whose video co-training directly shapes trajectory denoising, while the visibility mask prevents future-target leakage and enables trajectory-only inference.

Main result: From the abstract, UNIVERSE reaches 91.0 PDMS on NAVSIM versus 89.6 for a Two-DiT variant, reports zero-shot transfer to nuScenes and Bench2Drive without fine-tuning, and achieves a 4.3x speedup for trajectory-only inference compared with joint video-action rollout.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide code, data-release status, full baseline comparisons, statistical uncertainty, detailed zero-shot transfer numbers, or robustness evidence; DOI, venue, affiliations, and citation signals are also absent from the supplied metadata.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the query 'causality' because it frames future video prediction as supervision for scene dynamics and temporal causality, and proposes a causal visibility mask to avoid leakage between future video and trajectory targets.

Tags: autonomous-driving, world-action-models, diffusion-transformer, video-prediction, trajectory-planning, causality, zero-shot-transfer

Figure note: A useful figure would show the UNIVERSE single-DiT architecture, including historical context sharing, future video and trajectory token streams, and the Modality-Decoupling Visibility Mask that blocks future-target cross-attention.

TL;DR: The paper studies how to identify or bound central moments of individual causal effects using only marginal moment information about potential outcomes, aiming to characterize treatment-effect heterogeneity with weaker inputs than full marginal distributions.

Problem: Average causal effects hide the shape and heterogeneity of individual causal effects; the paper asks when central moments of the ICE distribution can be identified or bounded from limited potential-outcome information.

Method: It proposes identification and bounding results for central moments of individual causal effects based only on marginal central moments of each potential outcome, rather than full marginal potential-outcome distributions.

Key contribution: The main novelty claimed in the abstract is replacing the stronger requirement of knowing full marginal distributions of potential outcomes with the weaker and often more accessible requirement of knowing marginal moment information.

Main result: The abstract reports theoretical identification/bounding results and says they are illustrated with two empirical case studies, but it does not provide quantitative empirical findings or benchmark comparisons.

item.title: Learning 4D Geometric Priors for Inference-Efficient World Action Models
item.description:

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper in cs.RO and cs.AI, published 2026-07-06.; Abstract claims MECo-WAM injects action-relevant 4D geometric priors into video-action representations.; Training uses a lightweight 4D expert supervised by relational targets from a frozen VGGT encoder.; Asymmetric expert visibility is claimed to prevent non-causal shortcuts.

TL;DR: MECo-WAM trains world action models for robotic manipulation with auxiliary 4D geometric priors while removing the geometry branch at deployment to preserve inference cost.

Problem: Existing video-action co-training for World Action Models tends to optimize appearance-oriented video latents, which may miss temporally evolving geometry needed for precise robotic manipulation.

Method: The paper proposes MECo-WAM, a multi-expert co-training framework combining video and action experts with a lightweight 4D geometry expert supervised by relational targets from a frozen VGGT encoder, using asymmetric expert visibility, decayed 4D read-mask attention, and action-aware temporal geometric distillation.

Key contribution: The novelty claimed in the abstract is transferring action-relevant 4D geometric knowledge into the deployed video-action pathway during training, while removing all auxiliary 4D components at inference so manipulation gains do not add inference cost.

Main result: The abstract reports 98.2% on LIBERO, 92.6% on RoboTwin 2.0, and improvements on challenging real-world manipulation tasks, but does not provide baseline numbers or statistical detail.

Limitation: The abstract does not mention code release, ablations, statistical robustness, baseline comparisons, exact real-world task setup, or whether the causal masking design was isolated experimentally.

Relevance: Relevant to the 'causality' query because the method explicitly uses asymmetric expert visibility to prevent non-causal shortcuts from auxiliary geometry to action generation, though the paper is primarily about robotic world action models rather than causal inference theory.

Tags: robotics, world-action-models, 4d-geometry, geometric-distillation, causal-masking, manipulation, video-action-co-training

item.title: Do Vision-Language-Action Models Mean What They Say? On the Role of Faithfulness in Embodied Reasoning
item.description:
Priority: 60/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 28, Paper 50, Empirical 78, Reproducibility 45, Manuscript 76

Missing quality signals: No DOI, journal, or conference venue in metadata., No affiliations, citation counts, h-index, or prior author-topic metadata supplied., No code repository or license stated, only a project page URL., No dataset names, benchmark details, metric definitions, ablations, or statistical robustness in the abstract., No explicit data availability or implementation details in the supplied metadata., No direct limitation discussion visible in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper in cs.RO and cs.AI, published 2026-07-06.; Abstract distinguishes functional reasoning from faithful reasoning in embodied Chain-of-Thought.; Reports human evaluation of a SoTA autonomous-driving reasoning model.; Proposes Pinocchio, a learned critic scoring observation grounding and stepwise coherence.

TL;DR: The paper studies whether embodied Chain-of-Thought in VLA driving models is causally faithful to policy decisions and proposes a learned faithfulness critic, Pinocchio, to improve grounded and coherent reasoning through RL post-training.

Problem: Embodied Chain-of-Thought can improve interpretability and performance in robot/VLA policies, but the abstract argues that verbal reasoning may be functionally useful without faithfully reflecting the policy's internal decision process, especially when intermediate reasoning is ungrounded, inconsistent, or confounded.

Method: The authors distinguish functional from faithful reasoning, evaluate a state-of-the-art autonomous-driving reasoning model with human judgments, train a critic called Pinocchio to score observation grounding and stepwise coherence, and use that critic as a dense reward for reinforcement-learning post-training of an embodied planner.

Key contribution: A behavioral surrogate for embodied reasoning faithfulness that targets causal alignment between observations, reasoning steps, and actions, rather than only optimizing trajectory error or standard alignment objectives.

Main result: Across withheld driving benchmarks, the post-trained planner improves faithfulness by 4% over a SoTA alignment baseline and 18% over a trajectory-error post-training baseline while maintaining competitive downstream performance; on synthetic OOD counterfactual tests, it improves responsiveness to rare scenarios by 1.6x relative to a SoTA policy.

Limitation: The abstract does not report dataset names, exact metrics, model architecture details, ablations, statistical uncertainty, code availability, or whether the learned critic itself is causally validated beyond behavioral scoring.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query "causality" because it explicitly studies whether VLA reasoning traces preserve causal links to action prediction and proposes a reward-based method to improve faithfulness under confounding and counterfactual scenarios.

Tags: causality, vision-language-action models, embodied reasoning, chain-of-thought, robotics, autonomous driving, reinforcement learning, faithfulness

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper's framework diagram showing how Pinocchio scores observation grounding and stepwise coherence, then supplies dense rewards for RL post-training of the embodied planner.

item.title: Retroactive Chain-of-Thought (RetroCoT): Forensic Reconstruction Prompts as a Safety Diagnostic Across Model Generations
item.description:
Priority: 48/100 (TL;DR only, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 24, Paper 35, Empirical 65, Reproducibility 25, Manuscript 68

Missing quality signals: author affiliation, citation count or h-index, DOI, journal or conference venue, code repository, full prompts or implementation details

Score evidence: arXiv v1 published 2026-07-06 with no DOI or journal reference in supplied metadata.; Single listed author: Samira Hajizadeh; no affiliation or citation metadata supplied.; Abstract reports AdvBench n=50 and direct-request baselines for GPT-4o and GPT-4o-mini.; Abstract reports GPT-5-family single-turn refusal and multi-turn continuation/control ASR results.

TL;DR: The paper studies how LLM safety refusals vary under semantically equivalent but pragmatically reframed harmful requests, introducing RetroCoT as a forensic-reconstruction prompting diagnostic.

Problem: Current safety alignment evaluations usually test direct harmful requests, but the paper argues that models may comply when the same harmful intent is framed in a different pragmatic register.

Method: The paper proposes Retroactive Chain-of-Thought, a single-turn prompt framing harmful objectives as forensic reverse reconstruction of an outcome that has already occurred, and evaluates it on AdvBench across GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-5-family models, plus adversarial feedback/control turns.

Key contribution: Its main novelty is treating pragmatic register invariance as a safety diagnostic and showing that forensic reconstruction framing can bypass models that refuse direct requests, while newer models appear to cover that specific register but remain vulnerable to related continuation framing.

Main result: From the abstract: RetroCoT reaches 58% ASR on GPT-4o and 52% on GPT-4o-mini versus 0% and 4% direct-request baselines; GPT-5-family models refuse single-turn RetroCoT, but an adversarial feedback/continuation turn raises ASR to 48% on GPT-5.4-mini and 94% on GPT-4o, with a control reaching 85% on GPT-5.4-mini.

Limitation: Evidence is limited to the abstract: no code, prompt set, full model list, statistical uncertainty, robustness analysis, affiliation, DOI, venue, or external replication is supplied. The abstract is truncated, so some methodology and limitation details are unavailable.

Relevance: Matched the query "causality" because the proposed framing asks models to reconstruct causal chains behind harmful outcomes; it is more directly relevant to LLM safety, adversarial prompting, and causal/pragmatic reasoning than to core causal inference.

Tags: LLM safety, adversarial prompting, jailbreak diagnostics, pragmatic framing, causal reconstruction, AdvBench

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework diagram contrasting direct harmful request evaluation with RetroCoT forensic reverse-causal-chain framing, plus the adversarial continuation/control flow across model generations.

item.title: A General Exposure-Mapping-Agnostic Framework for Causal Inference under Interference
item.description:
Priority: 66/100 (Skim, confidence: medium)

Quality dimensions: Author 50, Paper 68, Empirical 70, Reproducibility 45, Manuscript 82

Missing quality signals: Affiliations not supplied., Citation count and h-index signals not supplied., No DOI, journal, or conference reference supplied., No code repository or implementation artifact mentioned., No public data source mentioned., No exact simulation metrics, sample sizes, or numerical results in the abstract.

Score evidence: arXiv v1 paper published 2026-07-06 in stat.ME.; Abstract states the framework avoids exposure mapping assumptions, exclusion of cross-cluster interference, and Bernoulli treatment assignments.; Abstract claims a complete characterization of linear weighted estimators for identifying network causal effects under interference.; Abstract reports central limit theorems and conservative variance estimators for most estimators.

TL;DR: The paper develops an exposure-mapping-agnostic design-based framework for causal inference under network interference in two-stage cluster-randomized experiments, with a characterization of linear weighted estimators and supporting asymptotic and simulation evidence.

Problem: It studies how to identify and estimate network causal effects under interference in clustered experiments when units are connected across a network and standard simplifying assumptions such as fixed exposure mappings, no cross-cluster interference, or Bernoulli treatment assignment may fail.

Method: The paper proposes a general design-based framework for two-stage randomization on networks and characterizes linear weighted estimators, in the Godambe sense, that identify causal effects under interference. It also derives central limit theorems and conservative variance estimators for most estimators in the class.

Key contribution: The main novelty is an exposure-mapping-agnostic framework that covers cluster experiments with network interference and non-Bernoulli assignments, plus new linear weighted estimators including cluster-agnostic estimators whose convergence rates are independent of the number of clusters and can attain root-N rates.

Main result: According to the abstract, the new estimators have improved theoretical guarantees and superior finite-sample performance over standard inverse-probability-of-treatment weighting, supported by extensive simulations across interference structures. Exact numerical gains are not provided in the abstract.

Limitation: The abstract does not report code, public data, exact simulation settings, quantitative effect sizes, real-data validation, ablations, robustness checks, DOI, venue, or affiliations. Limitations are not explicitly discussed.

Relevance: Highly relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' because it directly addresses causal effect identification and estimation under interference, a central problem in modern causal inference for networked and clustered experiments.

Tags: causal inference, interference, cluster randomized experiments, network causal effects, design-based inference, linear weighted estimators, two-stage randomization, asymptotic theory

Figure note: A useful figure would show the two-stage cluster-randomized network setting, how interference crosses clusters, and where the proposed linear weighted estimator subclasses fit relative to IPTW; no direct image URL is available from the supplied metadata.
